blocks.html: III Smooth morphisms: extension properties

pages.title: Formally smooth homomorphisms
pages.html:
1 Formally smooth homomorphisms

In II, we restricted ourselves to the consideration of homomorphisms of finite type, and consequently, among the local homomorphisms \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) of local rings, to the case where \(B\) is isomorphic to a localized algebra of an \(A\)-algebra of finite type. This case is insufficient for various applications, notably in formal geometry or in analytic geometry. For example, the ring of formal power series \(B=A[[t_1,\dots,t_n]]\) has (from the point of view of formal geometry) the properties of a smooth algebra over \(A\). In analytic geometry, the same holds for the local ring of a point \((y,z)\) of a product \(Y\times\mathbf{C}^n\) considered as an algebra over the local ring of \(y\); moreover, the completion of this algebra is isomorphic to the algebra of formal power series in \(n\) indeterminates over the completion of the base ring \(\mathscr{O}_x\). This is what leads us to lay down the following definition.

Definition 1

Let \(u\colon A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) be a local homomorphism of local rings (Noetherian, we recall). One supposes \(\mathit{k}(B)\) finite over \(\mathit{k}(A)\). One says that \(u\) is a formally smooth homomorphism, or that the algebra \(B\) is formally smooth over \(A\), if there exists a local finite \(\overline{A}\)-algebra \(A'\), free over \(\overline{A}\), such that the local components of the semi-local ring \(\overline{B}\otimes_{\overline{A}} A'=B'\) are \(A'\)-isomorphic to algebras of formal power series over \(A'\)1.

(One denotes by \(\overline{A}\), \(\overline{B}\) the completed rings of \(A\), \(B\)). Since \(B'\) is finite and free over \(\overline{B}\), it is indeed a semi-local ring, a direct composite of complete local rings, each of which is again a free module over \(\overline{B}\), hence has the same dimension as \(\overline{B}\) hence as \(B\). It follows that the number of variables \(t_i\) in the rings of formal power series envisaged in 1 is equal to \(\dim \overline{B}-\dim \overline{A}=\dim B-\dim A\), and in particular independent of the local component chosen. One sees at once that this is also the dimension of the ring \(B\otimes k=B/\mathfrak{m} B\), where \(k=A/\mathfrak{m}\) is the residue field of \(A\); it will be called the relative dimension of \(B\) with respect to \(A\).

Remarks 2

It is evident that the definition 1 depends only on the homomorphism on the completions \(\overline{A} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \overline{B}\) deduced from \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\), which justifies to a certain extent the terminology. We here repent of the definition I 2 b) and I 1 b), which risks inducing error, and we prefer to say “formally un- ramified” and “formally étale” in the cases envisaged in these definitions, reserving the terminology “unramified” and “étale” for the case where \(B\) is a localization of an \(A\)-algebra of finite type2. The reader will verify at once that “formally étale” is equivalent to “formally smooth and quasi-finite”. Finally, let us point out that there is a reasonable definition of “formally smooth” without any prior hypothesis on the residual extension \(\mathit{k}(B)/\mathit{k}(A)\) (here assumed finite), encompassing among others the local homomorphisms \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) such that \(B\) is flat over \(A\) and \(B/\mathfrak{m}B\) a separable extension of \(A/\mathfrak{m} =k\) (not necessarily of finite type); for example, a Cohen \(p\)-ring is formally smooth over the ring of \(p\)-adic integers. It is the property of lifting of homomorphisms (compare 1) that must become the definition in this general case. For the applications we have in view, the case treated in the definition 1 will suffice for us; in what follows, in “formally smooth” we shall understand “with finite residual extension”.

Lemma 3

If \(B\) is formally smooth over \(A\), \(B\) is flat over \(A\).

Since flatness is invariant under completion, one may suppose \(A\) and \(B\) complete. Since flatness is invariant under a flat (hence faithfully flat) local extension of the base ring, one is reduced, by virtue of definition 1, to the case where \(B\) is an algebra of formal power series over \(A\). But then, as an \(A\)-module, \(B\) is isomorphic to a product of \(A\)-modules isomorphic to \(A\), hence (the base ring \(A\) being Noetherian) is \(A\)-flat as a product of flat \(A\)-modules.

Let us place ourselves under the conditions of 1. Since the residual extensions of the local components of \(B'\) over \(A'\) are trivial, it follows that \(L\otimes_k k'\) is an Artinian \(k'\)-algebra whose local components have trivial residual extensions (where \(L,\, k,\, k'\) are the residue fields of \(A,\, B,\, A'\)). This condition, necessary in order that the finite free extension \(A'\) satisfy the condition stated in 1, is also sufficient, as follows at once from 4 (i) and 5 below.

Proposition 4

Let \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) be a local homomorphism of local rings, with finite residual extension, and let \(A'\) be a finite local \(A\)-algebra over \(A\), so that \(B'=B\otimes_A A'\) is finite over \(B\), hence a semi-local ring likewise Noetherian. (i) If \(B\) is formally smooth over \(A\), then the localizations of B at its maximal ideals are formally smooth over \(A'\). (ii) The converse is true if \(A'\) is free over \(A\).

One is at once reduced to the case where \(A\), \(B\) are complete.

(i) Let \(A''\) be a finite free local extension of \(A\) such that the local components of \(B''=B\otimes_A A''\) are algebras of formal power series over \(A''\). Making the extension of scalars \(A''\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A''\otimes_A A' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A'''\), where \(A'''\) is a local component of \(A''\otimes_A A'\), one sees that the local components of \(B''\otimes_{A''} A'''=B\otimes_A A'''\) are algebras of formal power series over \(A'''\). Now one also has \(B\otimes_AA'''=(B\otimes_A A')\otimes_{A'} A'''=B'\otimes_{A'} A'''\), on the other hand, since \(A''\) is free over \(A\), \(A''\otimes_A A'\) is free over \(A'\) and the same holds consequently for \(A'''\) which is a direct factor of it, which proves that \(B'\) is formally smooth over \(A'\).

(ii) Let \(A''\) be a finite free local \(A'\)-algebra such that the local components of \(B'\otimes_{A'} A'' = B\otimes_A A''\) are algebras of formal power series over \(A''\). Since \(A'\) is free over \(A\), \(A''\) is so too, hence \(B\) is formally smooth over \(A\).

Proposition 5

Let \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) be a local homomorphism of local rings, with trivial residual extension. In order that \(B\) be formally smooth over \(A\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(\overline{B}\) be isomorphic to an algebra of formal power series over \(\overline{A}\).

Only the necessity remains to be proved, and one may suppose \(A\) and \(B\) complete. Let \(\mathfrak{m}\) (\(\mathfrak{n}\)) be the maximal ideal of \(A\) (\(B\)) and let \(t_1,\dots, t_n\) be elements of \(\mathfrak{n}\) which define a basis of the vector space

\[ (\mathfrak{n}/\mathfrak{n}^2)/ \operatorname{Im} (\mathfrak{m}/\mathfrak{m}^2)= \mathfrak{n}/(\mathfrak{n}^2 +\mathfrak{m}B) \]

These elements thus define a homomorphism of local \(A\)-algebras

\[ B_1= A[[t_1,\dots,t_n]] \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B \]

let us prove that it is an isomorphism. It suffices to prove that for every power \(\mathfrak{m}^q\) of \(\mathfrak{m}\), one obtains an isomorphism upon reducing mod \(\mathfrak{m}^q\) (since \(B_1\) and \(B\) are the inverse limits of the corresponding rings reduced mod \(\mathfrak{m}^q\), \(q\) variable). Since \(B\) and \(B_1\) are flat \(A\)-modules, so that the graded rings associated with the \(\mathfrak{m}\)-adic filtration are obtained by tensoring \(\operatorname{gr} (A)\), over \(k=A/\mathfrak{m}\), with the rings \(B_1/\mathfrak{m}B_1\) resp. \(B/\mathfrak{m}B\), we are reduced to showing that \(B_1/\mathfrak{m}B_1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B/\mathfrak{m}B\) is an isomorphism. Taking into account 3, we are thus reduced to the case where \(A\) is a field \(k\). On the other hand, if \(A'\) is a finite free local \(A\)-algebra such that \(B\otimes_A A'\) is a formal power series algebra over \(A'\) (N.B. this algebra is local, since the residual extension of \(B\) over \(A\) is trivial), to prove that \(B_1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) is an isomorphism, it suffices to prove that \(B_1\otimes_A A'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\otimes_A A'\) is one. This reduces us to the case where \(B\) is already a formal power series algebra (this reduction had to be carried out first, before reducing to the case of a base field). But then \(B\) is a regular local ring with field of representatives \(k\), and it is well known (and immediate by consideration of the graded rings associated with the \(\mathfrak{n}_1 \)-adic and \(\mathfrak{n}\)-adic filtrations on \(B_1\) and \(B\)) that \(B_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) is an isomorphism, which completes the proof.

Corollary 6

If \(B\) is formally smooth over \(A\), then there exists a finite local \(A\)-algebra \(A'\) such that the local components of \(\overline{B}\otimes_{\overline{A}} \overline{A'}=\overline{(B\otimes_A A')}\) are isomorphic to formal power series algebras over \(\overline{A'}\).

Indeed, if \(L/k\) is the residual extension of \(B/A\), one considers an extension \(k'/k\) such that the residual extensions in the \(k'\)-algebra \(L\otimes_k k'\) are trivial. Let \(A'\) be a finite free \(A\)-algebra such that \(A'/\mathfrak{m}A'=k'\) (one knows that such an algebra exists, for example by reducing step by step to the case where \(k'/k\) is monogenic, and then one lifts to \(A\) the coefficients of the minimal polynomial of a generator of \(k'\) over \(k\)). It is local. Then \(B\otimes_A A'\) has at its maximal ideals residual extensions that are trivial above the residual extension \(k'\) of \(A'\), and one concludes with the help of 5.

Corollary 7

Let \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) be a local homomorphism of local rings. For \(B\) to be formally smooth over \(A\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(B\) be flat over \(A\) and that \(B/\mathfrak{m}B\) be formally smooth over \(k=A/\mathfrak{m}\).

Making a suitable finite free local extension \(A'\) of \(A\) and using 4 (ii), we are reduced to the case where the residual extension of \(B/A\) is trivial. We moreover know by 4 (i) and 3 that the conditions stated are necessary. For the sufficiency, it suffices to remark that the proof of 5 shows, under the hypotheses made here, that \(B\) is a formal power series algebra over \(A\) (assuming \(A\) and \(B\) complete, which is permissible).

Remark 8

It would not be difficult to develop, for formally smooth homomorphisms, the analogue of all the properties of smooth morphisms, studied in II. For the differential properties, this nevertheless requires a modification of the usual definition of Kähler differentials (cf. I 1), the completed tensor products replacing the ordinary tensor products. We content ourselves with evoking these abysses here, what precedes being sufficient for our purpose.

It remains to establish the connection between formal smoothness and the notion of smoothness developed in II (and of which we have as yet made no use):

Proposition 9

Let \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) be a local homomorphism, \(B\) being a localization of an \(A\)-algebra of finite type. For \(B\) to be smooth over \(A\), it is necessary and sufficient that it be formally smooth over \(A\).

Using 7 and II 1, we are reduced to the case where \(A\) is a field.

Using 4 (ii) and II 13, a suitable extension \(k'\) of \(k\) reduces us to the case where the residual extension for \(B/k\) is trivial. By virtue of 5 (resp. II 2) \(B\) is then smooth over \(k\) (resp. formally smooth over \(k\)) if and only if \(B\) is a regular local ring (resp. its completion is a formal power series algebra over \(k\)). Now it is well known that these two conditions are equivalent (the residual extension being trivial).

(One denotes by \(\overline{A}\), \(\overline{B}\) the completed rings of \(A\), \(B\)). blocks.html:
Remarks 2

It is evident that the definition 1 depends only on the homomorphism on the completions \(\overline{A} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \overline{B}\) deduced from \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\), which justifies to a certain extent the terminology. blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3

If \(B\) is formally smooth over \(A\), \(B\) is flat over \(A\).

Since flatness is invariant under completion, one may suppose \(A\) and \(B\) complete. Since flatness is invariant under a flat (hence faithfully flat) local extension of the base ring, one is reduced, by virtue of definition 1, to the case where \(B\) is an algebra of formal power series over \(A\). But then, as an \(A\)-module, \(B\) is isomorphic to a product of \(A\)-modules isomorphic to \(A\), hence (the base ring \(A\) being Noetherian) is \(A\)-flat as a product of flat \(A\)-modules.

Let us place ourselves under the conditions of 1. Since the residual extensions of the local components of \(B'\) over \(A'\) are trivial, it follows that \(L\otimes_k k'\) is an Artinian \(k'\)-algebra whose local components have trivial residual extensions (where \(L,\, k,\, k'\) are the residue fields of \(A,\, B,\, A'\)). This condition, necessary in order that the finite free extension \(A'\) satisfy the condition stated in 1, is also sufficient, as follows at once from 4 (i) and 5 below.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4

Let \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) be a local homomorphism of local rings, with finite residual extension, and let \(A'\) be a finite local \(A\)-algebra over \(A\), so that \(B'=B\otimes_A A'\) is finite over \(B\), hence a semi-local ring likewise Noetherian. (i) If \(B\) is formally smooth over \(A\), then the localizations of B at its maximal ideals are formally smooth over \(A'\). (ii) The converse is true if \(A'\) is free over \(A\).
One is at once reduced to the case where \(A\), \(B\) are complete.

(i) Let \(A''\) be a finite free local extension of \(A\) such that the local components of \(B''=B\otimes_A A''\) are algebras of formal power series over \(A''\). Making the extension of scalars \(A''\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A''\otimes_A A' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A'''\), where \(A'''\) is a local component of \(A''\otimes_A A'\), one sees that the local components of \(B''\otimes_{A''} A'''=B\otimes_A A'''\) are algebras of formal power series over \(A'''\). Now one also has \(B\otimes_AA'''=(B\otimes_A A')\otimes_{A'} A'''=B'\otimes_{A'} A'''\), on the other hand, since \(A''\) is free over \(A\), \(A''\otimes_A A'\) is free over \(A'\) and the same holds consequently for \(A'''\) which is a direct factor of it, which proves that \(B'\) is formally smooth over \(A'\).

(ii) Let \(A''\) be a finite free local \(A'\)-algebra such that the local components of \(B'\otimes_{A'} A'' = B\otimes_A A''\) are algebras of formal power series over \(A''\). Since \(A'\) is free over \(A\), \(A''\) is so too, hence \(B\) is formally smooth over \(A\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 5

Let \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) be a local homomorphism of local rings, with trivial residual extension. In order that \(B\) be formally smooth over \(A\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(\overline{B}\) be isomorphic to an algebra of formal power series over \(\overline{A}\).

Only the necessity remains to be proved, and one may suppose \(A\) and \(B\) complete. Let \(\mathfrak{m}\) (\(\mathfrak{n}\)) be the maximal ideal of \(A\) (\(B\)) and let \(t_1,\dots, t_n\) be elements of \(\mathfrak{n}\) which define a basis of the vector space
\[ (\mathfrak{n}/\mathfrak{n}^2)/ \operatorname{Im} (\mathfrak{m}/\mathfrak{m}^2)= \mathfrak{n}/(\mathfrak{n}^2 +\mathfrak{m}B) \]
These elements thus define a homomorphism of local \(A\)-algebras
\[ B_1= A[[t_1,\dots,t_n]] \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B \]

let us prove that it is an isomorphism. It suffices to prove that for every power \(\mathfrak{m}^q\) of \(\mathfrak{m}\), one obtains an isomorphism upon reducing mod \(\mathfrak{m}^q\) (since \(B_1\) and \(B\) are the inverse limits of the corresponding rings reduced mod \(\mathfrak{m}^q\), \(q\) variable). Taking into account 3, we are thus reduced to the case where \(A\) is a field \(k\). On the other hand, if \(A'\) is a finite free local \(A\)-algebra such that \(B\otimes_A A'\) is a formal power series algebra over \(A'\) (N.B. this algebra is local, since the residual extension of \(B\) over \(A\) is trivial), to prove that \(B_1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) is an isomorphism, it suffices to prove that \(B_1\otimes_A A'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\otimes_A A'\) is one. This reduces us to the case where \(B\) is already a formal power series algebra (this reduction had to be carried out first, before reducing to the case of a base field). But then \(B\) is a regular local ring with field of representatives \(k\), and it is well known (and immediate by consideration of the graded rings associated with the \(\mathfrak{n}_1 \)-adic and \(\mathfrak{n}\)-adic filtrations on \(B_1\) and \(B\)) that \(B_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) is an isomorphism, which completes the proof.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6

If \(B\) is formally smooth over \(A\), then there exists a finite local \(A\)-algebra \(A'\) such that the local components of \(\overline{B}\otimes_{\overline{A}} \overline{A'}=\overline{(B\otimes_A A')}\) are isomorphic to formal power series algebras over \(\overline{A'}\).

Indeed, if \(L/k\) is the residual extension of \(B/A\), one considers an extension \(k'/k\) such that the residual extensions in the \(k'\)-algebra \(L\otimes_k k'\) are trivial. Let \(A'\) be a finite free \(A\)-algebra such that \(A'/\mathfrak{m}A'=k'\) (one knows that such an algebra exists, for example by reducing step by step to the case where \(k'/k\) is monogenic, and then one lifts to \(A\) the coefficients of the minimal polynomial of a generator of \(k'\) over \(k\)). It is local. Then \(B\otimes_A A'\) has at its maximal ideals residual extensions that are trivial above the residual extension \(k'\) of \(A'\), and one concludes with the help of 5.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 7

Let \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) be a local homomorphism of local rings. For \(B\) to be formally smooth over \(A\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(B\) be flat over \(A\) and that \(B/\mathfrak{m}B\) be formally smooth over \(k=A/\mathfrak{m}\).

Making a suitable finite free local extension \(A'\) of \(A\) and using 4 (ii), we are reduced to the case where the residual extension of \(B/A\) is trivial. We moreover know by 4 (i) and 3 that the conditions stated are necessary. For the sufficiency, it suffices to remark that the proof of 5 shows, under the hypotheses made here, that \(B\) is a formal power series algebra over \(A\) (assuming \(A\) and \(B\) complete, which is permissible).

blocks.html:

It would not be difficult to develop, for formally smooth homomorphisms, the analogue of all the properties of smooth morphisms, studied in II. For the differential properties, this nevertheless requires a modification of the usual definition of Kähler differentials (cf. I 1), the completed tensor products replacing the ordinary tensor products. We content ourselves with evoking these abysses here, what precedes being sufficient for our purpose.

It remains to establish the connection between formal smoothness and the notion of smoothness developed in II (and of which we have as yet made no use):

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 9

Let \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) be a local homomorphism, \(B\) being a localization of an \(A\)-algebra of finite type. For \(B\) to be smooth over \(A\), it is necessary and sufficient that it be formally smooth over \(A\).
Using 7 and II 1, we are reduced to the case where \(A\) is a field.

Using 4 (ii) and II 13, a suitable extension \(k'\) of \(k\) reduces us to the case where the residual extension for \(B/k\) is trivial. By virtue of 5 (resp. II 2) \(B\) is then smooth over \(k\) (resp. formally smooth over \(k\)) if and only if \(B\) is a regular local ring (resp. its completion is a formal power series algebra over \(k\)). pages.title:
Characteristic lifting property of
formally smooth homomorphisms
pages.html:
2 Characteristic lifting property of formally smooth homomorphisms

Theorem 1

Let \(A \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) be a local homomorphism of local rings defining a finite residue extension. The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    \(B\) is formally smooth over \(A\)

  2. (ii)

    For every local homomorphism \(A \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} C\), where \(C\) is a complete local ring, every ideal \(J\) of \(C\) contained in \(\mathfrak{r}(C)\), and every local \(A\)-homomorphism \(B \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} C/J\), there exists an \(A\)-homomorphism (necessarily local) \(B \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} C\) which lifts it.

  3. (iii)

    For every \(A\)-algebra \(C\) (not necessarily a Noetherian ring), every nilpotent ideal \(J\) of \(C\), and every \(A\)-homomorphism \(B \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} C\) that is continuous (i.e. vanishing on a power of \(\mathfrak{r}(B)\)), there exists an \(A\)-homomorphism \(B \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} C\) (necessarily continuous as well) which lifts it.

  4. (iv)

    The same statement as (ii) and (iii), but \(C\) being an Artinian local ring, finite over \(A\).

  5. (iv bis)

    As (iv), but \(J\) being moreover of square zero.

Remark

For the sequel of this exposé, we shall use only the implication (iv)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(i) or (iv bis)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(i); the direct implication (i)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(ii) will be proved by another method in the next section when \(B\) is a localization of an algebra of finite type over \(A\). Let us recall that in the “good” theory of Cohen's theorems1, property (ii) or (iii) becomes the definition of formally smooth homomorphisms, whereas 1 becomes a characteristic property valid only in the case of a finite residue extension. One will note that of the properties (ii) and (iii) neither is more general than the other. One could give an (equivalent) property covering both, by introducing a linearly topologized ring \(C\), separated and complete, a closed topologically nilpotent ideal of \(C\), and a continuous homomorphism \(A \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} C\) (thus making \(C\) a topological \(A\)-algebra); we leave this modification to the reader.

Proof of 1

We shall prove (i)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(iii)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(ii), then (iv)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(i). Since (ii)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(iv) is trivial, and the equivalence of (iv) and (iv bis) is seen by an immediate induction on the integer \(n\) such that \(J^n = 0\), this will complete the proof.

(i)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(iii). An immediate induction reduces us to the case where \(J^2 = 0\). Since \(C\) is finite over \(A\), there exists a power \(\mathfrak{m}^q\) of the maximal ideal of \(A\) which annihilates \(C\). Dividing by \(\mathfrak{m}^q\), and noting that \(B/\mathfrak{m}^qB\) is still formally smooth over \(A/\mathfrak{m}^q\) by 4 (i), we may assume \(A\) Artinian. Since \(B\) is flat over \(A\) by 3, \(B\) is free over \(A\) since \(A\) is Artinian. Hence there exists a homomorphism of \(A\)-modules

\[ w \colon B \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} C \]

which lifts the given homomorphism \(u \colon B \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} C/J\). Set

\[ f(x,y) = w(xy) - w(x)w(y) \qquad (x,y \in B) \]

then \(f(x,y) \in J\), and \(f\) is therefore an \(A\)-bilinear map of \(B \times B\) into \(J\). For there to exist a lifting \(v \colon B \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} C\) of \(u\) that is an algebra homomorphism, it is necessary and sufficient that there exist an \(A\)-linear map \(g \colon B \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} J\) such that \(v = w + g\) be an algebra homomorphism, which is written

\begin{align*} g(1) &= 1 - w(1) \\ g(x,y) - u(x)g(y) -u(y)g(x) &= -f(x,y) \qquad (\text{for } x,y \in B) \end{align*}

This is a system of linear equations in \(\operatorname{Hom}_A(B,J)\), with right-hand sides in \(J\), so it has a solution if and only if the corresponding system in \(\operatorname{Hom}_A(B,J) \otimes_A A'\), with right-hand sides in \(J' = J \otimes_A A'\), has a solution, — \(A'\) denoting a faithfully flat algebra over \(A\). Now let \(A'\) be a finite and free algebra over \(A\), local, such that \(B' = B \otimes_A A'\) is an algebra of formal power series over \(A'\) (N.B. in our proof we may assume \(A\) and \(B\) complete, as one checks at once). Since \(A'\) is free of finite type over \(A\), we have

\[ \operatorname{Hom}_A(B,J) \otimes_A A' = \operatorname{Hom}_{A'}(B',J') \]

and one checks that the system of equations obtained in \(\operatorname{Hom}_{A'}(B',J')\) is the one which determines the homomorphisms of \(A'\)-algebras \(B' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} C' = C \otimes_A A'\) which lift the homomorphism \(u' \colon B' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} C'/J'\) deduced from \(u\) by extension of scalars, by “correcting” by a homomorphism of \(A'\)-modules \(g' \colon B' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} J'\) the homomorphism of \(A'\)-modules \(w' \colon B' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} C'\) deduced from \(w\) by extension of scalars. (Note that \(B\) generates \(B'\) as an \(A'\)-module). We are thus reduced to proving (iii) when \(B\) is an algebra of formal power series over \(A\), \(B = A[[t_1, \dots, t_n]]\). Then let us lift in an arbitrary way the images in \(C/J\) of the \(t_i\) to elements \(z_i\) of \(C\). Since the \(z_i\) mod \(J\) are nilpotent (\(u \colon B \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} C/J\) being continuous) so are the \(z_i\) (since \(J\) is nilpotent), hence the \(z_i\) define a continuous homomorphism of topological \(A\)-algebras of \(B\) into \(C\) discrete, evidently lifting \(u\), q.e.d.

(iii)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(ii). Let \(\mathfrak{n}\) be the maximal ideal of \(C\), and for every integer \(q > 0\), let

\[ C_q = C/\mathfrak{n}^q \text{, \quad} J_q = (J + \mathfrak{n}^q)/\mathfrak{n}^q \]

so \(C_q/J_q\) is identified with a quotient algebra of \(C/J\), and on the other hand the composite homomorphism \(u_q \colon B \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} C/J \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} C_q/J_q\) is continuous from \(B\) into \(C_q/J_q\) discrete, and \(J_q\) is a nilpotent ideal in \(C_q\). One then constructs step by step \(A\)-homomorphisms

\[ v_q \colon B \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} C_q \]

such that (a) \(v_q\) lifts \(u_q\) and (b) \(v_q\) lifts \(v_{q-1}\). The possibility of the induction is verified easily, for since

\[ u_q \colon B \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} C/(J + \mathfrak{n}^q) \text{\quad and \quad} v_{q-1} \colon B \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} C/\mathfrak{n}^{q-1} \]

define the same homomorphism

\[ B \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} C/((J + \mathfrak{n}^q) + \mathfrak{n}^{q-1}) = C/(J + \mathfrak{n}^{q-1}) = C_{q-1}/J_{q-1} \]

namely \(u_{q-1}\), they define a homomorphism

\[ B \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} C/J_q' \text{\quad where \quad} J_q' = (J + \mathfrak{n}^q)\cap \mathfrak{n}^{q-1} \supset \mathfrak{n}^q \]

(from which both of them arise by reduction). We are thus reduced to lifting a homomorphism \(B \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} C/J_q'\) of \(B\) into a quotient of \(C_q\) by an ideal \(J_q'/\mathfrak{n}^q\) contained in \(J_q\), hence nilpotent, and this is possible according to hypothesis (iii).

This done, the \(v_q\) define a homomorphism of \(B\) into the inverse limit \(C\) of the \(C_q\). Since \(J\) is closed, \(J\) is the inverse limit of the \(J_q\), hence \(v\) lifts \(u\), q.e.d.

(iv)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(i). We first observe immediately that if (iv) is satisfied, (iv) remains satisfied for the local components of \(B \otimes_A A'\) over \(A'\), if \(A'\) is a finite local algebra over \(A\). Taking \(A'\) free over \(A\) and such that the residue extensions of \(B'\) above \(A'\) are trivial, we are reduced, thanks to 4 (ii), to the case where the residue extension of \(B\) over \(A\) is trivial. We shall then prove the somewhat more precise result:

Corollary 2

Under the conditions of 1, suppose moreover that the residue extension of \(B\) above \(A\) is trivial. Then the equivalent conditions of 1 are also equivalent to the following two conditions (assuming in (v) that \(A\) and \(B\) are complete):

  1. (iv ter)

    Like (iv), but with the Artinian local ring \(C\) finite over \(A\) restricted to having a trivial residue extension (and moreover, if one insists, with the ideal \(J\) being of square zero).

  2. (v)

    There exists a local \(A\)-homomorphism (where \(n = \dim{\mathfrak{n}/(\mathfrak{n}^2+\mathfrak{m}B})\))

    \[ u \colon B \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B_1 = A[[t_1, \dots, t_n]] \]

    inducing an isomorphism

    \[ \mathfrak{n}/(\mathfrak{n}^2+\mathfrak{m}B) \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathfrak{n}_1/(\mathfrak{n}_1^2+\mathfrak{m}B_1) \]

    where \(\mathfrak{n}\) (\(\mathfrak{n}_1\)) is the maximal ideal of \(B\) (\(B_1\)), and \(\mathfrak{m}\) that of \(A\).

Proof

Since (iv bis) obviously implies (iv ter) — disregarding the joke of the square-zero ideal —, it will suffice to prove (iv ter)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(v)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(i).

(iv ter)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(v). Let us choose a basis \(a_1,\dots,a_n\) of \(\mathfrak{n}/(\mathfrak{n}^2+\mathfrak{m}B)\), which thus defines a local homomorphism of \(A\)-algebras

\[ B \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B_1/(\mathfrak{n}_1^2+\mathfrak{m}B_1) = k[t_1, \dots, t_n]/(t_1, \dots, t_n)^2 \]

which can be lifted step by step, by virtue of (iv ter), to homomorphisms of \(A\)-algebras from \(B\) into \(B_1/\mathfrak{n}_1^2\), \(B_1/\mathfrak{n}_1^3\), etc., whence, on passing to the inverse limit, the homomorphism \(B \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B_1\) having the desired property.

(v)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(i). Since in the commutative diagram

\[ \xymatrix{{\mathfrak{m}/\mathfrak{m}^2}\ar[r]\ar[d]&{\mathfrak{n}/\mathfrak{n}^2}\ar[r]\ar[d]&{\mathfrak{n}/(\mathfrak{n}^2+\mathfrak{m}B)}\ar[r]\ar[d]&{0}\\{\mathfrak{m}/\mathfrak{m}^2}\ar[r]&{\mathfrak{n}_1/\mathfrak{n}_1^2}\ar[r]&{\mathfrak{n}_1/(\mathfrak{n}_1^2+\mathfrak{m}B_1)}\ar[r]&{0}} \]

the two rows are exact, and the extreme vertical arrows surjective, the middle arrow is surjective and it follows (\(B\) being complete) that \(B \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B_1\) is surjective. Let \(x_i\) (\(1 \leqslant i \leqslant n\)) be elements of \(B\) which lift the \(t_i\). They thus define a homomorphism of \(A\)-algebras \(B_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\), which will be surjective for the same reason as \(u\), and whose composite with \(u\) is the identity by construction. Hence \(B_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) is also injective, and is consequently an isomorphism. We thus find

Corollary 3

Under the conditions of 2 (v), \(u\) is necessarily an isomorphism.

This completes the proof that \(B\) is formally smooth over \(A\). We have moreover at the same time recovered 5 (though there is scarcely any merit in that).

blocks.html: 2 Characteristic lifting property of formally smooth homomorphisms

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 1

Let \(A \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) be a local homomorphism of local rings defining a finite residue extension. The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    \(B\) is formally smooth over \(A\)

  2. (ii)

    For every local homomorphism \(A \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} C\), where \(C\) is a complete local ring, every ideal \(J\) of \(C\) contained in \(\mathfrak{r}(C)\), and every local \(A\)-homomorphism \(B \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} C/J\), there exists an \(A\)-homomorphism (necessarily local) \(B \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} C\) which lifts it.

  3. (iii)

    For every \(A\)-algebra \(C\) (not necessarily a Noetherian ring), every nilpotent ideal \(J\) of \(C\), and every \(A\)-homomorphism \(B \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} C\) that is continuous (i.e. vanishing on a power of \(\mathfrak{r}(B)\)), there exists an \(A\)-homomorphism \(B \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} C\) (necessarily continuous as well) which lifts it.

  4. (iv)

    The same statement as (ii) and (iii), but \(C\) being an Artinian local ring, finite over \(A\).

  5. (iv bis)

    As (iv), but \(J\) being moreover of square zero.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark

For the sequel of this exposé, we shall use only the implication (iv)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(i) or (iv bis)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(i); the direct implication (i)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(ii) will be proved by another method in the next section when \(B\) is a localization of an algebra of finite type over \(A\). Let us recall that in the “good” theory of Cohen's theorems1, property (ii) or (iii) becomes the definition of formally smooth homomorphisms, whereas 1 becomes a characteristic property valid only in the case of a finite residue extension. One will note that of the properties (ii) and (iii) neither is more general than the other. One could give an (equivalent) property covering both, by introducing a linearly topologized ring \(C\), separated and complete, a closed topologically nilpotent ideal of \(C\), and a continuous homomorphism \(A \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} C\) (thus making \(C\) a topological \(A\)-algebra); we leave this modification to the reader.
Proof of 1

We shall prove (i)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(iii)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(ii), then (iv)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(i). Since (ii)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(iv) is trivial, and the equivalence of (iv) and (iv bis) is seen by an immediate induction on the integer \(n\) such that \(J^n = 0\), this will complete the proof.

(i)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(iii). An immediate induction reduces us to the case where \(J^2 = 0\). Since \(C\) is finite over \(A\), there exists a power \(\mathfrak{m}^q\) of the maximal ideal of \(A\) which annihilates \(C\). Dividing by \(\mathfrak{m}^q\), and noting that \(B/\mathfrak{m}^qB\) is still formally smooth over \(A/\mathfrak{m}^q\) by 4 (i), we may assume \(A\) Artinian. Since \(B\) is flat over \(A\) by 3, \(B\) is free over \(A\) since \(A\) is Artinian. Hence there exists a homomorphism of \(A\)-modules
\[ w \colon B \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} C \]
which lifts the given homomorphism \(u \colon B \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} C/J\). Set
\[ f(x,y) = w(xy) - w(x)w(y) \qquad (x,y \in B) \]

then \(f(x,y) \in J\), and \(f\) is therefore an \(A\)-bilinear map of \(B \times B\) into \(J\). For there to exist a lifting \(v \colon B \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} C\) of \(u\) that is an algebra homomorphism, it is necessary and sufficient that there exist an \(A\)-linear map \(g \colon B \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} J\) such that \(v = w + g\) be an algebra homomorphism, which is written
\begin{align*} g(1) &= 1 - w(1) \\ g(x,y) - u(x)g(y) -u(y)g(x) &= -f(x,y) \qquad (\text{for } x,y \in B) \end{align*}

This is a system of linear equations in \(\operatorname{Hom}_A(B,J)\), with right-hand sides in \(J\), so it has a solution if and only if the corresponding system in \(\operatorname{Hom}_A(B,J) \otimes_A A'\), with right-hand sides in \(J' = J \otimes_A A'\), has a solution, — \(A'\) denoting a faithfully flat algebra over \(A\). Now let \(A'\) be a finite and free algebra over \(A\), local, such that \(B' = B \otimes_A A'\) is an algebra of formal power series over \(A'\) (N.B. in our proof we may assume \(A\) and \(B\) complete, as one checks at once). Since \(A'\) is free of finite type over \(A\), we have
\[ \operatorname{Hom}_A(B,J) \otimes_A A' = \operatorname{Hom}_{A'}(B',J') \]

and one checks that the system of equations obtained in \(\operatorname{Hom}_{A'}(B',J')\) is the one which determines the homomorphisms of \(A'\)-algebras \(B' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} C' = C \otimes_A A'\) which lift the homomorphism \(u' \colon B' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} C'/J'\) deduced from \(u\) by extension of scalars, by “correcting” by a homomorphism of \(A'\)-modules \(g' \colon B' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} J'\) the homomorphism of \(A'\)-modules \(w' \colon B' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} C'\) deduced from \(w\) by extension of scalars. (Note that \(B\) generates \(B'\) as an \(A'\)-module). We are thus reduced to proving (iii) when \(B\) is an algebra of formal power series over \(A\), \(B = A[[t_1, \dots, t_n]]\). Then let us lift in an arbitrary way the images in \(C/J\) of the \(t_i\) to elements \(z_i\) of \(C\). Since the \(z_i\) mod \(J\) are nilpotent (\(u \colon B \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} C/J\) being continuous) so are the \(z_i\) (since \(J\) is nilpotent), hence the \(z_i\) define a continuous homomorphism of topological \(A\)-algebras of \(B\) into \(C\) discrete, evidently lifting \(u\), q.e.d.
(iii)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(ii). Let \(\mathfrak{n}\) be the maximal ideal of \(C\), and for every integer \(q > 0\), let
\[ C_q = C/\mathfrak{n}^q \text{, \quad} J_q = (J + \mathfrak{n}^q)/\mathfrak{n}^q \]

so \(C_q/J_q\) is identified with a quotient algebra of \(C/J\), and on the other hand the composite homomorphism \(u_q \colon B \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} C/J \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} C_q/J_q\) is continuous from \(B\) into \(C_q/J_q\) discrete, and \(J_q\) is a nilpotent ideal in \(C_q\). One then constructs step by step \(A\)-homomorphisms
\[ v_q \colon B \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} C_q \]
such that (a) \(v_q\) lifts \(u_q\) and (b) \(v_q\) lifts \(v_{q-1}\). The possibility of the induction is verified easily, for since

\[ u_q \colon B \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} C/(J + \mathfrak{n}^q) \text{\quad and \quad} v_{q-1} \colon B \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} C/\mathfrak{n}^{q-1} \]
define the same homomorphism
\[ B \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} C/((J + \mathfrak{n}^q) + \mathfrak{n}^{q-1}) = C/(J + \mathfrak{n}^{q-1}) = C_{q-1}/J_{q-1} \]
namely \(u_{q-1}\), they define a homomorphism
\[ B \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} C/J_q' \text{\quad where \quad} J_q' = (J + \mathfrak{n}^q)\cap \mathfrak{n}^{q-1} \supset \mathfrak{n}^q \]

(from which both of them arise by reduction). We are thus reduced to lifting a homomorphism \(B \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} C/J_q'\) of \(B\) into a quotient of \(C_q\) by an ideal \(J_q'/\mathfrak{n}^q\) contained in \(J_q\), hence nilpotent, and this is possible according to hypothesis (iii).
This done, the \(v_q\) define a homomorphism of \(B\) into the inverse limit \(C\) of the \(C_q\). Since \(J\) is closed, \(J\) is the inverse limit of the \(J_q\), hence \(v\) lifts \(u\), q.e.d.

(iv)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(i). We first observe immediately that if (iv) is satisfied, (iv) remains satisfied for the local components of \(B \otimes_A A'\) over \(A'\), if \(A'\) is a finite local algebra over \(A\). Taking \(A'\) free over \(A\) and such that the residue extensions of \(B'\) above \(A'\) are trivial, we are reduced, thanks to 4 (ii), to the case where the residue extension of \(B\) over \(A\) is trivial. We shall then prove the somewhat more precise result:

blocks.html:
Corollary 2

Under the conditions of 1, suppose moreover that the residue extension of \(B\) above \(A\) is trivial. Then the equivalent conditions of 1 are also equivalent to the following two conditions (assuming in (v) that \(A\) and \(B\) are complete):

  1. (iv ter)

    Like (iv), but with the Artinian local ring \(C\) finite over \(A\) restricted to having a trivial residue extension (and moreover, if one insists, with the ideal \(J\) being of square zero).

  2. (v)

    There exists a local \(A\)-homomorphism (where \(n = \dim{\mathfrak{n}/(\mathfrak{n}^2+\mathfrak{m}B})\))

    \[ u \colon B \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B_1 = A[[t_1, \dots, t_n]] \]

    inducing an isomorphism

    \[ \mathfrak{n}/(\mathfrak{n}^2+\mathfrak{m}B) \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathfrak{n}_1/(\mathfrak{n}_1^2+\mathfrak{m}B_1) \]

    where \(\mathfrak{n}\) (\(\mathfrak{n}_1\)) is the maximal ideal of \(B\) (\(B_1\)), and \(\mathfrak{m}\) that of \(A\).
Proof

Since (iv bis) obviously implies (iv ter) — disregarding the joke of the square-zero ideal —, it will suffice to prove (iv ter)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(v)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(i).

(iv ter)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(v). Let us choose a basis \(a_1,\dots,a_n\) of \(\mathfrak{n}/(\mathfrak{n}^2+\mathfrak{m}B)\), which thus defines a local homomorphism of \(A\)-algebras
\[ B \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B_1/(\mathfrak{n}_1^2+\mathfrak{m}B_1) = k[t_1, \dots, t_n]/(t_1, \dots, t_n)^2 \]

which can be lifted step by step, by virtue of (iv ter), to homomorphisms of \(A\)-algebras from \(B\) into \(B_1/\mathfrak{n}_1^2\), \(B_1/\mathfrak{n}_1^3\), etc., whence, on passing to the inverse limit, the homomorphism \(B \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B_1\) having the desired property.

\[ \xymatrix{{\mathfrak{m}/\mathfrak{m}^2}\ar[r]\ar[d]&{\mathfrak{n}/\mathfrak{n}^2}\ar[r]\ar[d]&{\mathfrak{n}/(\mathfrak{n}^2+\mathfrak{m}B)}\ar[r]\ar[d]&{0}\\{\mathfrak{m}/\mathfrak{m}^2}\ar[r]&{\mathfrak{n}_1/\mathfrak{n}_1^2}\ar[r]&{\mathfrak{n}_1/(\mathfrak{n}_1^2+\mathfrak{m}B_1)}\ar[r]&{0}} \]

the two rows are exact, and the extreme vertical arrows surjective, the middle arrow is surjective and it follows (\(B\) being complete) that \(B \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B_1\) is surjective. Let \(x_i\) (\(1 \leqslant i \leqslant n\)) be elements of \(B\) which lift the \(t_i\). They thus define a homomorphism of \(A\)-algebras \(B_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\), which will be surjective for the same reason as \(u\), and whose composite with \(u\) is the identity by construction. Hence \(B_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) is also injective, and is consequently an isomorphism. We thus find

Corollary 3

Under the conditions of 2 (v), \(u\) is necessarily an isomorphism.
This completes the proof that \(B\) is formally smooth over \(A\). We have moreover at the same time recovered 5 (though there is scarcely any merit in that).

pages.title:
Local infinitesimal extension of morphisms into a
\(S\)-scheme smooth
pages.html:
3 Local infinitesimal extension of morphisms into a smooth \(S\)-scheme

Theorem 1

Let \(f \colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a morphism locally of finite type. Equivalent conditions:

  1. (i)

    \(f\) is smooth.

  2. (ii)

    For every prescheme \(Y'\) over \(Y\), every closed sub-prescheme \(Y'_0\) of \(Y'\) having the same underlying space as \(Y'\), every \(Y\)-morphism \(g_0 \colon Y'_0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) and every \(z \in Y'_0\), there exists an open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(z\) in \(Y'\) and an extension \(g\) of \(g_0|Y'_0 \cap U\) to a \(Y\)-morphism \(U \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\).

  3. (ii bis)

    For \(Y'\), \(Y'_0\) and \(z\) as in (ii), setting \(X' = X \times_Y Y'\), \(X'_0 = X \times_Y Y'_0\), every section of \(X'_0\) over \(Y'_0\) extends to a section of \(X'\) over an open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(z\).

  4. (iii)

    For every \(Y\)-scheme \(Y'\), the spectrum of an Artinian ring that is local and finite over some \(\mathscr{O}_y\) (\(y \in Y\)), every non-empty closed sub-prescheme \(Y'_0\) of \(Y'\), and every \(Y\)-morphism \(g_0 \colon Y'_0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), there exists a \(Y\)-morphism \(g \colon Y' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) which extends \(g_0\).

  5. (iii bis)

    For every \(Y'\), \(Y'_0\) as in (iii), setting \(X' = X \times_Y Y'\), \(X'_0 = X \times_Y Y'_0\), every section of \(X'_0\) over \(Y'_0\) extends to a section of \(X'\) over \(Y'\).

Proof

The equivalence of (ii) and (ii bis) on the one hand, and of (iii) and (iii bis) on the other, is trivial, as is the implication (ii)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(iii). It thus remains to prove (i)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(ii) and (iii)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(i).

(i)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(ii). Let \(x = g_0(z)\). Replacing \(X\) by a suitable open neighbourhood of \(x\), and \(Y'\) by the prescheme induced on the open inverse image of the latter under \(g_0\), one may assume that \(X\) is étale over \(Y[t_1, \dots, t_n]\). Consider the composite \(Y\)-morphism \(Y'_0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y[t_1, \dots, t_n]\); it is defined by \(n\) sections of the sheaf \(\mathscr{O}_{Y'_0}\), which can therefore be extended in a neighbourhood of \(z\) to sections of \(\mathscr{O}_{Y'}\), so that one may assume that the morphism in question has been extended to a \(Y\)-morphism \(Y' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y'_0\). By virtue of (I 6) there then exists a unique \(Y\)-morphism \(g \colon Y' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) which lifts the preceding one, and at the same time extends \(g_0\), q.e.d.

(iii)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(i). Since the set of points where \(f\) is smooth is open, it suffices to prove that it contains every \(x \in X\) which is closed in its fibre. Let \(y = f(x)\); then \(\mathscr{O}_x\) is an algebra over \(\mathscr{O}_y\), a localization of an algebra of finite type, with finite residue extension. On the other hand, hypothesis (iii) implies that every homomorphism of \(\mathscr{O}_x\) into an algebra \(A/J\), where \(A\) is a finite local Artinian algebra over \(\mathscr{O}_y\), and \(J\) an ideal contained in its radical, lifts to a homomorphism of \(\mathscr{O}_x\) into the algebra \(A\) (taking into account that a morphism of a \(\operatorname{Spec}(B)\), \(B\) a local ring, into \(X\) is determined biunivocally by a local homomorphism of some \(\mathscr{O}_x\) (\(x \in X\)) into \(B\)). By 1 it follows that \(\mathscr{O}_x\) is formally smooth over \(\mathscr{O}_y\), hence smooth over \(\mathscr{O}_y\) by virtue of 9.

Corollary 2

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be as in 1. Equivalent conditions:

  1. (i)

    \(f\) is étale

  2. (ii)

    One has condition (ii) of 1 with uniqueness of the extension \(g\) of \(g_0\) to \(U\).

  3. (iii)

    One has condition (iii) of 1 with uniqueness of \(g\).

It suffices to note, in the proof of (i)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(ii) above, that one can have uniqueness (when \(Y'_0\) is not identical to \(Y'\) in a neighbourhood of \(z\)) only if \(n=0\) (a condition which is known to be sufficient).

Corollary 3

Let \(X\) be a prescheme locally of finite type over a complete local ring \(A\), \(y\) the closed point of \(Y=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and \(x\) a point of \(f^{-1}(y)\) rational over \(\mathit{k}(y)\). If \(X\) is smooth over \(A\) at \(x\), then there exists a section \(s\) of \(X\) over \(Y\) “passing through \(x\)”, i.e. such that \(s(y)=x\).

In particular, if \(X\) is smooth over \(A\), then the natural map

\[ \Gamma(X/Y)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\Gamma(X\otimes_A k/k) \]

from the sections of \(X\) over \(Y\) into the set of points of the fibre \(f^{-1}(y)=X\otimes_A k\) rational over \(k\), is surjective. This fact was above all well known and used when \(A\) is a discrete valuation ring and \(X\) is proper over \(A\) (in fact, projective over \(A\)), in which case the sections of \(X\) over \(Y\) (i.e. the “points of \(X\) with values in \(A\)”) are also identified with the rational sections, i.e. with the points of \(X\otimes_A K=X_K\) (which is a proper and simple scheme over \(K\)) with values in \(K=\) field of fractions of \(A\), i.e. with the points of \(X\) rational over \(K\).

blocks.html: 3 Local infinitesimal extension of morphisms into a smooth \(S\)-scheme

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 1

Let \(f \colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a morphism locally of finite type. Equivalent conditions:

  1. (i)

    \(f\) is smooth.

  2. (ii)

    For every prescheme \(Y'\) over \(Y\), every closed sub-prescheme \(Y'_0\) of \(Y'\) having the same underlying space as \(Y'\), every \(Y\)-morphism \(g_0 \colon Y'_0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) and every \(z \in Y'_0\), there exists an open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(z\) in \(Y'\) and an extension \(g\) of \(g_0|Y'_0 \cap U\) to a \(Y\)-morphism \(U \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\).

  3. (ii bis)

    For \(Y'\), \(Y'_0\) and \(z\) as in (ii), setting \(X' = X \times_Y Y'\), \(X'_0 = X \times_Y Y'_0\), every section of \(X'_0\) over \(Y'_0\) extends to a section of \(X'\) over an open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(z\).

  4. (iii)

    For every \(Y\)-scheme \(Y'\), the spectrum of an Artinian ring that is local and finite over some \(\mathscr{O}_y\) (\(y \in Y\)), every non-empty closed sub-prescheme \(Y'_0\) of \(Y'\), and every \(Y\)-morphism \(g_0 \colon Y'_0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), there exists a \(Y\)-morphism \(g \colon Y' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) which extends \(g_0\).

  5. (iii bis)

    For every \(Y'\), \(Y'_0\) as in (iii), setting \(X' = X \times_Y Y'\), \(X'_0 = X \times_Y Y'_0\), every section of \(X'_0\) over \(Y'_0\) extends to a section of \(X'\) over \(Y'\).
Proof

The equivalence of (ii) and (ii bis) on the one hand, and of (iii) and (iii bis) on the other, is trivial, as is the implication (ii)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(iii). It thus remains to prove (i)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(ii) and (iii)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(i).

(i)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(ii). Let \(x = g_0(z)\). Replacing \(X\) by a suitable open neighbourhood of \(x\), and \(Y'\) by the prescheme induced on the open inverse image of the latter under \(g_0\), one may assume that \(X\) is étale over \(Y[t_1, \dots, t_n]\). Consider the composite \(Y\)-morphism \(Y'_0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y[t_1, \dots, t_n]\); it is defined by \(n\) sections of the sheaf \(\mathscr{O}_{Y'_0}\), which can therefore be extended in a neighbourhood of \(z\) to sections of \(\mathscr{O}_{Y'}\), so that one may assume that the morphism in question has been extended to a \(Y\)-morphism \(Y' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y'_0\). By virtue of (I 6) there then exists a unique \(Y\)-morphism \(g \colon Y' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) which lifts the preceding one, and at the same time extends \(g_0\), q.e.d.

(iii)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(i). Since the set of points where \(f\) is smooth is open, it suffices to prove that it contains every \(x \in X\) which is closed in its fibre. Let \(y = f(x)\); then \(\mathscr{O}_x\) is an algebra over \(\mathscr{O}_y\), a localization of an algebra of finite type, with finite residue extension. On the other hand, hypothesis (iii) implies that every homomorphism of \(\mathscr{O}_x\) into an algebra \(A/J\), where \(A\) is a finite local Artinian algebra over \(\mathscr{O}_y\), and \(J\) an ideal contained in its radical, lifts to a homomorphism of \(\mathscr{O}_x\) into the algebra \(A\) (taking into account that a morphism of a \(\operatorname{Spec}(B)\), \(B\) a local ring, into \(X\) is determined biunivocally by a local homomorphism of some \(\mathscr{O}_x\) (\(x \in X\)) into \(B\)). By 1 it follows that \(\mathscr{O}_x\) is formally smooth over \(\mathscr{O}_y\), hence smooth over \(\mathscr{O}_y\) by virtue of 9.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be as in 1. Equivalent conditions:

  1. (i)

    \(f\) is étale

  2. (ii)

    One has condition (ii) of 1 with uniqueness of the extension \(g\) of \(g_0\) to \(U\).

  3. (iii)

    One has condition (iii) of 1 with uniqueness of \(g\).

It suffices to note, in the proof of (i)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(ii) above, that one can have uniqueness (when \(Y'_0\) is not identical to \(Y'\) in a neighbourhood of \(z\)) only if \(n=0\) (a condition which is known to be sufficient).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3

Let \(X\) be a prescheme locally of finite type over a complete local ring \(A\), \(y\) the closed point of \(Y=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and \(x\) a point of \(f^{-1}(y)\) rational over \(\mathit{k}(y)\). If \(X\) is smooth over \(A\) at \(x\), then there exists a section \(s\) of \(X\) over \(Y\) “passing through \(x\)”, i.e. such that \(s(y)=x\).
In particular, if \(X\) is smooth over \(A\), then the natural map
\[ \Gamma(X/Y)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\Gamma(X\otimes_A k/k) \]

from the sections of \(X\) over \(Y\) into the set of points of the fibre \(f^{-1}(y)=X\otimes_A k\) rational over \(k\), is surjective. This fact was above all well known and used when \(A\) is a discrete valuation ring and \(X\) is proper over \(A\) (in fact, projective over \(A\)), in which case the sections of \(X\) over \(Y\) (i.e. the “points of \(X\) with values in \(A\)”) are also identified with the rational sections, i.e. with the points of \(X\otimes_A K=X_K\) (which is a proper and simple scheme over \(K\)) with values in \(K=\) field of fractions of \(A\), i.e. with the points of \(X\) rational over \(K\).

pages.title: Local infinitesimal extension of smooth \(S\)-schemes
pages.html:
4 Local infinitesimal extension of smooth \(S\)-schemes

Theorem 1

Let \(Y\) be a locally Noetherian prescheme, \(Y_0\) a closed sub­prescheme having the same underlying space, \(X_0\) a smooth \(Y_0\)-prescheme, \(x\) a point of \(X_0\). Then there exist an open neighbourhood \(U_0\) of \(x\), a prescheme \(X\) smooth over \(Y\), and a \(Y_0\)-isomorphism:

\[ h\colon U_0\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} X\times_Y Y_0. \]

Moreover, if \((U'_0,X',h')\) is another solution of this problem, then “it is isomorphic to the first in a neighbourhood of \(x\)”.

We leave it to the reader to make precise what is meant by this. One may note that for given \(U_0\), a solution of the posed problem amounts to the datum, over \(U_0\), of a sheaf of algebras \(\mathscr{B}\) over \(f_0^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_Y)\) (where \(f_0\) is the continuous map underlying the structural morphism \(U_0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y_0\)), equipped with a ring homomorphism \(\mathscr{B}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{O}_{U_0}\) compatible with the homomorphism \(f^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_Y)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_{Y_0})\), such that

(a) this homomorphism induces an isomorphism

\[ \mathscr{B}\otimes_{f^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_Y)} f^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_{Y_0})\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathscr{O}_{Y_0}; \]

(b) \(U_0\) equipped with \(\mathscr{B}\) becomes a smooth \(Y\)-prescheme.

In this way, the precise meaning of the assertion of local uniqueness becomes particularly evident.

Proof

We may already suppose that \(X_0\) is étale over some \(Y_0[t_1,\dots,t_n]=Y'_0\). Now this last may be considered as a closed sub-prescheme of \(Y'{=} Y[t_1,\dots,t_n]\), having the same underlying space. By (I 3), there exist an \(X\) étale over \(Y'\), and a \(Y'_0\)-isomorphism \(X\times_{Y'}Y'_0\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} X'\). We have won for existence. For uniqueness, we use property 1 (ii) of smooth morphisms, taking into account the

Lemma 2

Let \(Y\) be a prescheme, \(Y_0\) a closed sub-prescheme defined by a locally nilpotent sheaf of ideals \(\mathscr{J}\), \(X\) and \(X'\) \(Y\)-preschemes, \(u\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X'\) a \(Y\)-morphism. Suppose \(X\) flat over \(Y\). For \(u\) to be an isomorphism, it is necessary and sufficient that \(u_0\colon X\times_Y Y_0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X'\times_Y Y_0\) be one.

Easy proof, by passing to the affine case and looking at the associated graded objects. One will note moreover that the analogous statement, obtained by replacing “isomorphism” by “closed immersion”, is likewise valid, and without any hypothesis of flatness.

Remark 3

It is essential to note that the local extension \(X\) obtained in 1 is not canonical, in other words the local isomorphism between two solutions is not unique, i.e. there exist in general non-trivial \(Y\)-automorphisms of \(X\) inducing the identity on the closed sub-prescheme \(X_0=X\times_Y Y_0\). It is for this reason that one must expect, for the construction of global infinitesimal extensions of smooth preschemes, the existence of an obstruction of cohomological nature, which will be made precise below (6).
