pages.title: Global infinitesimal extension of morphisms
pages.html:
5 Global infinitesimal extension of morphisms

Let \(T\) be a topological space, \(\mathscr{G}\) a sheaf of groups on \(X\), \(\mathscr{P}\) a sheaf of sets on \(T\) on which \(\mathscr{G}\) operates (on the right, to fix ideas). One says that \(\mathscr{P}\) is formally principal homogeneous under \(\mathscr{G}\), if the well-known homomorphism

\[ \mathscr{G}\times \mathscr{P}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{P}\times \mathscr{P} \]

of sheaves of sets, deduced from the operations of \(\mathscr{G}\) on \(\mathscr{P}\), is an isomorphism. It amounts to the same to say that for every \(x\in T\), \(\mathscr{P}_x\) is empty or a principal homogeneous space under the ordinary group \(\mathscr{G}_x\), or also that for every open set \(U\) of \(T\), \(\mathscr{P}(U)\) is empty or a principal homogeneous space under the ordinary group \(\mathscr{G}(U)\). One says that \(\mathscr{P}\) is a principal homogeneous sheaf under \(\mathscr{G}\) if it is so formally and if moreover the \(\mathscr{P}_x\) are non-empty (in other words, if all the \(\mathscr{P}_x\) are principal homogeneous spaces, hence non-empty, under the \(\mathscr{G}_x\))1. Let us recall that the set of classes (up to isomorphism) of principal homogeneous sheaves under \(\mathscr{G}\) is identified with the cohomology set \(\mathrm{H}^1(T,\mathscr{G})\), which is also the usual cohomology group of \(T\) with coefficients in \(\mathscr{G}\) when \(\mathscr{G}\) is commutative. One thus has, for every principal homogeneous \(\mathscr{P}\), a characteristic class \(c(\mathscr{P})\in\mathrm{H}^1(T,\mathscr{G})\), whose triviality is necessary and sufficient for \(\mathscr{P}\) to be trivial (i.e. isomorphic to \(\mathscr{G}\), on which \(\mathscr{G}\) operates by right translations), or again for \(\mathscr{P}\) to have a section.

Proposition 1

Let \(S\) be a prescheme, \(X\) and \(Y\) preschemes over \(S\), \(Y_0\) a closed sub-prescheme of \(Y\) defined by an Ideal \(\mathscr{J}\) on \(Y\) of square zero. Let \(g_0\) be an \(S\)-morphism of \(Y_0\) into \(X\), and \(\mathscr{P}(g_0)\) the sheaf on \(Y\) whose sections over an open set \(U\) are the extensions \(g\colon U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) of \(g_0|U\cap Y_0\) into an \(S\)-morphism \(g\). Then \(\mathscr{P}(g_0)\) is a formally principal homogeneous sheaf (in a natural way) under the commutative sheaf of groups

\[ \mathscr{G}=\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}}(g_0^*(\mathit{\Omega}^1_{X/S}),\mathscr{J}) \]

Set \(\mathscr{P}=\mathscr{P}(g_0)\). We must define, for every open set \(U\) of \(Y\), a map

\[ \mathscr{P}(U)\times \mathscr{G}(U)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{P}(U) \]

in such a way that (a) for fixed \(g\in \mathscr{P}(U)\), the map \(s\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} gs\) of \(\mathscr{G}(U)\) into \(\mathscr{P}(U)\) is bijective (b) \(\mathscr{P}(U)\) becomes a set with group of operators \(\mathscr{G}(U)\) (c) the preceding maps are compatible with the restriction operators for an open set \(V\subset U\). The verification of (c) will be trivial, so for simplicity one may suppose \(U=Y\). The verification of (b) (which is, if one wishes, of local nature) will be left to the reader; we shall therefore restrict ourselves, for a given \(g\in \mathscr{P}(Y)\), to defining a natural bijection of \(\mathscr{G}(Y)\) onto \(\mathscr{P}(Y)\). So one assumes already given an \(S\)-morphism \(g\colon Y\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), and one seeks a canonical bijection

\[ \tag{*} \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}}(g_0^*(\mathit{\Omega}^1_{X/S}),\mathscr{J})\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathscr{P}(Y) \]

where \(\mathscr{P}(Y)\) is the set of \(S\)-morphisms \(g'\) of \(Y\) into \(X\) inducing the same morphism \(g_0\colon Y_0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) as \(g\). The datum of such a \(g'\) is equivalent to the datum of an \(S\)-morphism \(h\colon Y\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\times_S X\) such that \({\mathrm{pr}}_1\circ h=g\), and \(h\circ i=(g_0,g_0)\), where \({\mathrm{pr}}_1\colon X\times_S X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) is the first projection, \(i\colon Y_0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) the canonical immersion, and \((g_0,g_0)\colon Y_0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\times_S X\) is the morphism \(\Delta_{X/S}g_0\) with components \(g_0,g_0\):

\[ \xymatrix@C=2.2cm{{X\times_S X}\ar[d]_{{\mathrm{pr}}_1}&{Y_0}\ar[l]_-{h_0=(g_0,g_0)}\ar[d]^i\\{X}&{Y}\ar[l]_-g\ar@{-->}[lu]_-h} \]

Since \(h_0\) factors through the diagonal immersion \(\Delta_{X/S}\) and \(Y\) lies in the infinitesimal neighbourhood of order \(1\) of \(Y_0\) (i.e. \(\mathscr{J}^2=0\)), the \(h\) sought factor necessarily (in a unique way) through the infinitesimal neighbourhood of the first order of the diagonal, which is identified (as an \(X\)-prescheme by means of \({\mathrm{pr}}_1\)) with the spectrum \(X'\) of the sheaf of algebras \(\mathscr{O}_X+\mathit{\Omega}^1_{X/S}\) (where the second term is considered as an Ideal of square zero), the diagonal morphism \(X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X'\) corresponding to the canonical augmentation of this sheaf of algebras. Set \(Y'=X'\times_X Y\), \(Y'_0=Y'\times_Y Y_0=X'\times_X Y_0\), so that the \(h\) sought are in biunivocal correspondence with the sections \(u\) of \(Y'\) over \(Y\) which extend a given section \(u_0\) of \(Y'_0\) over \(Y_0\). One may moreover identify \(Y'\) with the spectrum of the sheaf of algebras on \(Y\) \(\mathscr{A}=g^*(\mathscr{O}_X+\mathit{\Omega}^1_{X/S})=\mathscr{O}_Y+ g^*(\mathit{\Omega}^1_{X/S})\), and \(Y'_0\) with the sheaf of algebras \(\mathscr{A}_0=\mathscr{A}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_Y}\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}=\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}+ g^*_0(\mathit{\Omega}^1_{X/S})\); then the section \(u_0\) is that defined by the canonical augmentation of \(A_0\) into \(\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}\). Thus \(\mathscr{P}(Y)\) is identified with the set of algebra homomorphisms \(\mathscr{A}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{O}_Y\) which induce the canonical augmentation \(\mathscr{A}_0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{O}_{Y_0}\). Now the algebra homomorphisms \(\mathscr{A}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{O}_Y\) correspond biunivocally to the Module homomorphisms \(\mathscr{M}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{A}\) (setting for simplicity \(\mathscr{M}=g^*(\mathit{\Omega}^1_{X/S})\)), and one is interested in those which induce the null homomorphism \(\mathscr{M}_0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{O}_{Y_0}\) (where \(\mathscr{M}_0=\mathscr{M}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_Y}\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}\)) i.e. which map \(\mathscr{M}\) into the ideal \(\mathscr{J}\) of the augmentation. One therefore finds the set \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_Y}(\mathscr{M},\mathscr{J})=\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}}(\mathscr{M}_0,\mathscr{J})\) (since \(\mathscr{J}\) is annihilated by \(\mathscr{J}\)). This is the canonical bijection sought (*).

Taking account of the implication (i)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(iii) in 1, one finds:

Corollary 2

If \(X\) is smooth over \(S\) (at least at the points of \(g_0(Y_0)\)) then \(\mathscr{P}\) is even a principal homogeneous sheaf under the commutative sheaf of groups \(\mathscr{G}\), which in this case can also be written

\[ \mathscr{G}=g_0^*(\mathfrak{g}_{X/S})\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}}\mathscr{J} \]

where \(\mathfrak{g}_{X/S}\) is the sheaf on \(X\) dual to \(\mathit{\Omega}_{X/S}^1\), i.e. the tangent sheaf (or sheaf of derivations) of \(X\) relative to \(S\). (This last formula comes from the fact that \(\mathit{\Omega}_{X/S}^1\) is then free of finite type).

In particular, to this principal homogeneous sheaf there corresponds a cohomology class in \(\mathrm{H}^1(Y_0,\mathscr{G})\), whose vanishing is necessary and sufficient for the existence of an \(S\)-morphism \(g\) extending \(g_0\). And if such an extension exists, the set of all possible extensions is a homogeneous space under the group \(\mathrm{H}^0(Y_0,\mathscr{G})\).

In the application of the methods of formal geometry, the situation is most often the following: one is given two \(S\)-preschemes \(X\) and \(Y\), a coherent Ideal \(\mathscr{I}\) on \(S\); one denotes by \(S_n\) the closed sub-prescheme of \(S\) defined by \(\mathscr{I}^{n+1}\), and one sets

\[ X_n=X\times_S S_n ,\quad Y_n=Y\times_S S_n. \]

One supposes that one has an \(S_n\)-morphism:

\[ g_n\colon Y_n\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_n \]

(or, what amounts to the same, an \(S\)-morphism \(Y_n\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) or again an \(S_{n+1}\)-morphism \(Y_n\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_{n+1}\), since such a morphism necessarily induces \(Y_n\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_n\)), and one seeks to extend it into an \(S_{n+1}\)-morphism

\[ g_{n+1}\colon Y_{n+1}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_{n+1} \]

(If one can continue indefinitely, one thus obtains a morphism \(\widehat{Y}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\widehat{X}\) for the formal preschemes completed from \(Y\) and \(X\) for the Ideals \(\mathscr{IO}_Y\) and \(\mathscr{IO}_X\)). One may apply 1, replacing therein \((S,X,Y,Y_0,g_0)\) by \((S_{n+1},X_{n+1},Y_{n+1},Y_n,g_n)\); the sheaf \(\mathscr{G}\) becomes here the sheaf of Module homomorphisms from \(g_n^*(\mathit{\Omega}_{X_{n+1}/S_{n+1}}^1)\) into \(\mathscr{J}=\mathscr{I}^{n+1}\mathscr{O}_Y/\mathscr{I}^{n+2}\mathscr{O}_Y\). Since \(\mathscr{J}\) is annihilated by \(\mathscr{IO}_Y\), one may then replace \(g_n^*(\mathit{\Omega}_{X_{n+1}/S_{n+1}}^1)\) by the sheaf it induces on \(Y_0\), namely \(h_0^*(\mathit{\Omega}_{X/S}^1)\), where \(h_0\) is the composite \(Y_0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y_n\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_{n+1}\), or again the composite \(Y_0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_{n+1}\), where \(g_0\colon Y_0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_0\) is induced by \(g_n\). Since the inverse image of \(\mathit{\Omega}_{X_{n+1}/S_{n+1}}^1\) on \(X_0=X_{n+1}\times_{S_{n+1}}S_0\) is \(\mathit{\Omega}_{X_{0}/S_{0}}^1\), one sees that one also has

\[ \mathscr{G}=\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}} (g_0^*(\mathit{\Omega}_{X_0/S_0}^1), \mathscr{I}^{n+1}\mathscr{O}_Y/\mathscr{I}^{n+2}\mathscr{O}_Y) \]

Thus one obtains the

Corollary 3

Let \(S,X,Y,\mathscr{I},g_n\) be as above, let \(\mathscr{P}(g_n)\) be the sheaf on \(Y\) whose sections over an open \(U\) are the prolongations \(g_{n+1}\) of \(g_n\) into an \(S_{n+1}\)-morphism \(Y_{n+1}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_{n+1}\). Then \(\mathscr{P}(g_{n})\) is a formally principal homogeneous sheaf under the sheaf of groups

\[ \mathscr{G}=\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}} (g_0^*(\mathit{\Omega}_{X_0/S_0}^1), \operatorname{gr}_{\mathscr{IO}_Y}^{n+1}(\mathscr{O}_Y)) \]

In particular:

Corollary 4

If moreover \(X\) is smooth over \(S\) (at least at the points of \(g_0(Y_0)\)) then \(\mathscr{P}(g_{n})\) is even a principal homogeneous sheaf. .5cmIn particular, it defines an obstruction class in \(\mathrm{H}^1(Y_0,\mathscr{G})\), whose vanishing is necessary and sufficient for the existence of a global prolongation \(g_{n+1}\) of \(g_n\). And if such a prolongation exists, the set of all global prolongations is a principal homogeneous space under \(\mathrm{H}^0(Y_0,\mathscr{G})\). Finally, in the case considered, the sheaf \(\mathscr{G}\) can also be written

\[ \mathscr{G}=g_0^*(\mathfrak{g}_{X_0/S_0})\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}} \operatorname{gr}_{\mathscr{IO}_Y}^{n+1}(\mathscr{O}_Y) \]

Proceeding step by step, one thus sees that if all the \(\mathrm{H}^1(Y_0,\mathscr{G}_n)\) are zero (where \(\mathscr{G}_n=g_0^*(\mathfrak{g}_{X_0/S_0})\otimes \operatorname{gr}_{\mathscr{IO}_Y}^{n}(\mathscr{O}_Y)\)), then, starting with an arbitrary \(g_k\), one can successively prolong it into \(g_{k+1},\dots\) In particular, if \(\mathscr{I}\) is nilpotent, one will be able to find a prolongation \(g\) of \(g_k\) to \(Y\). The condition of vanishing of the \(\mathrm{H}^1\) is satisfied in particular if \(Y_0\) is affine. One thus finds:

Corollary 5

In the statement of Theorem 1, one obtains a necessary and sufficient condition equivalent to the others by assuming that the \(Y^\prime\) which occurs in (ii) (or (ii bis)) is affine, and by requiring the existence of a global prolongation \(g\) of \(g_0\) to all of \(Y^\prime\).

One will note that the proof of 1 could not give us this result directly.

An important case is that in which \(Y\) is flat over \(S\); then one has therefore

\[ \operatorname{gr}^n(\mathscr{O}_Y)=\operatorname{gr}^n(\mathscr{O}_S)\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}}\mathscr{O}_{Y_0} \]

and when moreover the \(\operatorname{gr}^n(\mathscr{O}_S)\) are locally free over \(S\), one finds

\[ \mathscr{G}_n= \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}}(g_0^*(\mathit{\Omega}_{X_0/S_0}^1),\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}) \otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}}\operatorname{gr}^n(\mathscr{O}_S), \]

or again, if \(\mathit{\Omega}_{X_0/S_0}^1\) is likewise locally free (for example \(X\) smooth over \(S\))

\[ \mathscr{G}_n=g_0^*(\mathfrak{g}_{X_0/S_0}) \otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}}\operatorname{gr}^{n}(\mathscr{O}_S) \]

If for example \(S\) is affine with affine ring \(A\), \(\mathscr{I}\) being defined by an ideal \(I\) of \(A\), one finds

\[ \mathrm{H}^i(Y_0,\mathscr{G}_n)=\mathrm{H}^i(Y_0,\mathscr{G}_0) \otimes_A\operatorname{gr}_I^n(A) \]

for every \(i\) (indeed, the question is local on \(S_0\), and one is reduced to the case where one tensors by a free Module). In this case, the vanishing of \(\mathrm{H}^1(Y_0,\mathscr{G}_0)\) implies that all the obstructions to the successive prolongations of \(g_n\) are zero. One thus obtains:

Corollary 6

Let \((S,X,Y,\mathscr{I},g_n)\) be as above, suppose moreover \(X\) smooth over \(S\) and \(Y\) flat over \(S\), finally \(S\) affine, and the \(\operatorname{gr}^n(\mathscr{O}_S)=\mathscr{I}^n/\mathscr{I}^{n+1}\) locally free. Then the obstruction to constructing \(g_{n+1}\) lies in \(\mathrm{H}^1(Y_0,\mathscr{G}_0)\otimes_A\operatorname{gr}_I^{n+1}(A)\) (where \(A\) is the ring of \(S\), \(I\) the ideal of \(A\) defining \(\mathscr{I}\)), setting

\[ \mathscr{G}_0=g_0^*(\mathfrak{g}_{X_0/S_0}) \]

If \(\mathrm{H}^1(Y_0,\mathscr{G}_0)=0\), then \(g_n\) can be prolonged into an \(\widehat{S}\)-morphism \(\widehat{g}\colon\widehat{Y}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\widehat{X}\).

Of course, this result would remain valid as it stands if one started, instead of ordinary \(S\)-preschemes \(X\) and \(Y\), from formal \(\widehat{\mathscr{I}}\)-adic \(\widehat{S}\)-preschemes \(\mathfrak{X}\) and \(\mathfrak{Y}\). It allows one to prove for example that certain proper formal schemes over a complete local ring (for example) are in fact algebraic. Indeed, proceeding as in Lemma 2, one finds:

Corollary 7

Under the conditions of 6, if \(g_0\) is an isomorphism the same holds for \(\widehat{g}\).

(N.B. the same result holds for closed immersions).

One thus obtains:

Proposition 8

Let \(A\) be a complete local ring with maximal ideal \(\mathfrak{m}\), with residue field \(k\), let \(\mathfrak{X}\) and \(\mathfrak{Y}\) be two formal \(\mathfrak{m}\)-adic preschemes over \(A\), flat over \(A\) (i.e. for every \(n\), \(X_n\) and \(Y_n\) are flat over \(A_n=A/\mathfrak{m}^{n+1}\)); suppose \(X_0=\mathfrak{X}\otimes_Ak\) smooth over \(k\) and \(\mathrm{H}^1(X_0,\mathfrak{g}_{X_0/k})=0\). Then every \(k\)-isomorphism of \(Y_0\) onto \(X_0\) prolongs into an \(A\)-isomorphism of \(\mathfrak{Y}\) onto \(\mathfrak{X}\); this prolongation is unique if moreover \(\mathrm{H}^0(X_0,\mathfrak{g}_{X_0/k})=0\).

This gives in particular a uniqueness result for a smooth formal prescheme over \(A\) reducing along a given prescheme \(X_0\) (provided \(\mathrm{H}^1(X_0,\mathfrak{g}_{X_0/k})=0\)). Moreover, if \(\mathfrak{X}\) and \(\mathfrak{Y}\) come from ordinary schemes proper over \(A\), say \(X\) and \(Y\), then one knows, by the existence theorem for sheaves in formal Geometry (cf. the talk at the Séminaire Bourbaki, 1822), that there is a bijective correspondence between the \(A\)-isomorphisms \(Y\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} X\) and the \(A\)-isomorphisms of the formal completions, hence

Corollary 9

The preceding statement 8 remains valid on replacing therein \(\mathfrak{X}\) and \(\mathfrak{Y}\) by ordinary \(A\)-schemes \(X\) and \(Y\), proper over \(A\).

Finally, when \(\mathfrak{X}\) is a formal scheme proper over \(A\), and \(\mathfrak{Y}\) is of the form \(\widehat{Y}\) where \(Y\) is an ordinary scheme proper over \(A\), then proposition 8 gives sufficient conditions for one to be able to find an isomorphism of \(\mathfrak{X}\) with \(\widehat{Y}\), hence for the formal scheme \(\mathfrak{X}\) to be in fact “algebraic” (i.e. isomorphic to a \(\widehat{X}\), \(X\) an ordinary scheme proper over \(A\), which will then be canonically determined). This is what happens in particular if \(X_0=\mathbf{P}_k^r\) (or more generally, if \(X_0\) is a Severi-Brauer scheme, i.e. becomes isomorphic to the standard projective space over the algebraic closure of \(k\)): every formal scheme proper and flat over \(A\), with fibre \(\mathbf{P}_k^r\), is algebraizable, and more precisely is isomorphic to the formal \(\mathfrak{m}\)-adic completion of \(\mathbf{P}_A^r\). In particular (thanks to the “existence theorem”) every ordinary scheme proper over \(A\), with fibre \(\mathbf{P}_k^r\), is isomorphic to \(\mathbf{P}_A^r\) (\(A\) being a complete local ring). Using the theory of descent, one can prove that if \(A\) is not complete, \(X\) becomes isomorphic to \(\mathbf{P}^r\) upon making a finite étale extension \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A^\prime\) of the base (and in this form, the result remains valid for a fibre that is a Severi-Brauer scheme).

blocks.html: 5 Global infinitesimal extension of morphisms

Let \(T\) be a topological space, \(\mathscr{G}\) a sheaf of groups on \(X\), \(\mathscr{P}\) a sheaf of sets on \(T\) on which \(\mathscr{G}\) operates (on the right, to fix ideas). One says that \(\mathscr{P}\) is formally principal homogeneous under \(\mathscr{G}\), if the well-known homomorphism
\[ \mathscr{G}\times \mathscr{P}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{P}\times \mathscr{P} \]

of sheaves of sets, deduced from the operations of \(\mathscr{G}\) on \(\mathscr{P}\), is an isomorphism. It amounts to the same to say that for every \(x\in T\), \(\mathscr{P}_x\) is empty or a principal homogeneous space under the ordinary group \(\mathscr{G}_x\), or also that for every open set \(U\) of \(T\), \(\mathscr{P}(U)\) is empty or a principal homogeneous space under the ordinary group \(\mathscr{G}(U)\). One says that \(\mathscr{P}\) is a principal homogeneous sheaf under \(\mathscr{G}\) if it is so formally and if moreover the \(\mathscr{P}_x\) are non-empty (in other words, if all the \(\mathscr{P}_x\) are principal homogeneous spaces, hence non-empty, under the \(\mathscr{G}_x\))1. Let us recall that the set of classes (up to isomorphism) of principal homogeneous sheaves under \(\mathscr{G}\) is identified with the cohomology set \(\mathrm{H}^1(T,\mathscr{G})\), which is also the usual cohomology group of \(T\) with coefficients in \(\mathscr{G}\) when \(\mathscr{G}\) is commutative. One thus has, for every principal homogeneous \(\mathscr{P}\), a characteristic class \(c(\mathscr{P})\in\mathrm{H}^1(T,\mathscr{G})\), whose triviality is necessary and sufficient for \(\mathscr{P}\) to be trivial (i.e. isomorphic to \(\mathscr{G}\), on which \(\mathscr{G}\) operates by right translations), or again for \(\mathscr{P}\) to have a section.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1

Let \(S\) be a prescheme, \(X\) and \(Y\) preschemes over \(S\), \(Y_0\) a closed sub-prescheme of \(Y\) defined by an Ideal \(\mathscr{J}\) on \(Y\) of square zero. Let \(g_0\) be an \(S\)-morphism of \(Y_0\) into \(X\), and \(\mathscr{P}(g_0)\) the sheaf on \(Y\) whose sections over an open set \(U\) are the extensions \(g\colon U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) of \(g_0|U\cap Y_0\) into an \(S\)-morphism \(g\). Then \(\mathscr{P}(g_0)\) is a formally principal homogeneous sheaf (in a natural way) under the commutative sheaf of groups

\[ \mathscr{G}=\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}}(g_0^*(\mathit{\Omega}^1_{X/S}),\mathscr{J}) \]
Set \(\mathscr{P}=\mathscr{P}(g_0)\). We must define, for every open set \(U\) of \(Y\), a map
\[ \mathscr{P}(U)\times \mathscr{G}(U)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{P}(U) \]

in such a way that (a) for fixed \(g\in \mathscr{P}(U)\), the map \(s\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} gs\) of \(\mathscr{G}(U)\) into \(\mathscr{P}(U)\) is bijective (b) \(\mathscr{P}(U)\) becomes a set with group of operators \(\mathscr{G}(U)\) (c) the preceding maps are compatible with the restriction operators for an open set \(V\subset U\). The verification of (c) will be trivial, so for simplicity one may suppose \(U=Y\). The verification of (b) (which is, if one wishes, of local nature) will be left to the reader; we shall therefore restrict ourselves, for a given \(g\in \mathscr{P}(Y)\), to defining a natural bijection of \(\mathscr{G}(Y)\) onto \(\mathscr{P}(Y)\). So one assumes already given an \(S\)-morphism \(g\colon Y\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), and one seeks a canonical bijection
\[ \tag{*} \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}}(g_0^*(\mathit{\Omega}^1_{X/S}),\mathscr{J})\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathscr{P}(Y) \]

where \(\mathscr{P}(Y)\) is the set of \(S\)-morphisms \(g'\) of \(Y\) into \(X\) inducing the same morphism \(g_0\colon Y_0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) as \(g\). The datum of such a \(g'\) is equivalent to the datum of an \(S\)-morphism \(h\colon Y\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\times_S X\) such that \({\mathrm{pr}}_1\circ h=g\), and \(h\circ i=(g_0,g_0)\), where \({\mathrm{pr}}_1\colon X\times_S X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) is the first projection, \(i\colon Y_0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) the canonical immersion, and \((g_0,g_0)\colon Y_0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\times_S X\) is the morphism \(\Delta_{X/S}g_0\) with components \(g_0,g_0\):
\[ \xymatrix@C=2.2cm{{X\times_S X}\ar[d]_{{\mathrm{pr}}_1}&{Y_0}\ar[l]_-{h_0=(g_0,g_0)}\ar[d]^i\\{X}&{Y}\ar[l]_-g\ar@{-->}[lu]_-h} \]

Since \(h_0\) factors through the diagonal immersion \(\Delta_{X/S}\) and \(Y\) lies in the infinitesimal neighbourhood of order \(1\) of \(Y_0\) (i.e. \(\mathscr{J}^2=0\)), the \(h\) sought factor necessarily (in a unique way) through the infinitesimal neighbourhood of the first order of the diagonal, which is identified (as an \(X\)-prescheme by means of \({\mathrm{pr}}_1\)) with the spectrum \(X'\) of the sheaf of algebras \(\mathscr{O}_X+\mathit{\Omega}^1_{X/S}\) (where the second term is considered as an Ideal of square zero), the diagonal morphism \(X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X'\) corresponding to the canonical augmentation of this sheaf of algebras. Set \(Y'=X'\times_X Y\), \(Y'_0=Y'\times_Y Y_0=X'\times_X Y_0\), so that the \(h\) sought are in biunivocal correspondence with the sections \(u\) of \(Y'\) over \(Y\) which extend a given section \(u_0\) of \(Y'_0\) over \(Y_0\). One may moreover identify \(Y'\) with the spectrum of the sheaf of algebras on \(Y\) \(\mathscr{A}=g^*(\mathscr{O}_X+\mathit{\Omega}^1_{X/S})=\mathscr{O}_Y+ g^*(\mathit{\Omega}^1_{X/S})\), and \(Y'_0\) with the sheaf of algebras \(\mathscr{A}_0=\mathscr{A}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_Y}\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}=\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}+ g^*_0(\mathit{\Omega}^1_{X/S})\); then the section \(u_0\) is that defined by the canonical augmentation of \(A_0\) into \(\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}\). Thus \(\mathscr{P}(Y)\) is identified with the set of algebra homomorphisms \(\mathscr{A}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{O}_Y\) which induce the canonical augmentation \(\mathscr{A}_0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{O}_{Y_0}\). Now the algebra homomorphisms \(\mathscr{A}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{O}_Y\) correspond biunivocally to the Module homomorphisms \(\mathscr{M}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{A}\) (setting for simplicity \(\mathscr{M}=g^*(\mathit{\Omega}^1_{X/S})\)), and one is interested in those which induce the null homomorphism \(\mathscr{M}_0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{O}_{Y_0}\) (where \(\mathscr{M}_0=\mathscr{M}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_Y}\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}\)) i.e. which map \(\mathscr{M}\) into the ideal \(\mathscr{J}\) of the augmentation. One therefore finds the set \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_Y}(\mathscr{M},\mathscr{J})=\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}}(\mathscr{M}_0,\mathscr{J})\) (since \(\mathscr{J}\) is annihilated by \(\mathscr{J}\)). This is the canonical bijection sought (*).
Taking account of the implication (i)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(iii) in 1, one finds:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2

If \(X\) is smooth over \(S\) (at least at the points of \(g_0(Y_0)\)) then \(\mathscr{P}\) is even a principal homogeneous sheaf under the commutative sheaf of groups \(\mathscr{G}\), which in this case can also be written

\[ \mathscr{G}=g_0^*(\mathfrak{g}_{X/S})\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}}\mathscr{J} \]

where \(\mathfrak{g}_{X/S}\) is the sheaf on \(X\) dual to \(\mathit{\Omega}_{X/S}^1\), i.e. the tangent sheaf (or sheaf of derivations) of \(X\) relative to \(S\). (This last formula comes from the fact that \(\mathit{\Omega}_{X/S}^1\) is then free of finite type).

In particular, to this principal homogeneous sheaf there corresponds a cohomology class in \(\mathrm{H}^1(Y_0,\mathscr{G})\), whose vanishing is necessary and sufficient for the existence of an \(S\)-morphism \(g\) extending \(g_0\). And if such an extension exists, the set of all possible extensions is a homogeneous space under the group \(\mathrm{H}^0(Y_0,\mathscr{G})\).

In the application of the methods of formal geometry, the situation is most often the following: one is given two \(S\)-preschemes \(X\) and \(Y\), a coherent Ideal \(\mathscr{I}\) on \(S\); one denotes by \(S_n\) the closed sub-prescheme of \(S\) defined by \(\mathscr{I}^{n+1}\), and one sets
\[ X_n=X\times_S S_n ,\quad Y_n=Y\times_S S_n. \]
One supposes that one has an \(S_n\)-morphism:
\[ g_n\colon Y_n\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_n \]

(or, what amounts to the same, an \(S\)-morphism \(Y_n\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) or again an \(S_{n+1}\)-morphism \(Y_n\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_{n+1}\), since such a morphism necessarily induces \(Y_n\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_n\)), and one seeks to extend it into an \(S_{n+1}\)-morphism
\[ g_{n+1}\colon Y_{n+1}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_{n+1} \]

(If one can continue indefinitely, one thus obtains a morphism \(\widehat{Y}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\widehat{X}\) for the formal preschemes completed from \(Y\) and \(X\) for the Ideals \(\mathscr{IO}_Y\) and \(\mathscr{IO}_X\)). One may apply 1, replacing therein \((S,X,Y,Y_0,g_0)\) by \((S_{n+1},X_{n+1},Y_{n+1},Y_n,g_n)\); the sheaf \(\mathscr{G}\) becomes here the sheaf of Module homomorphisms from \(g_n^*(\mathit{\Omega}_{X_{n+1}/S_{n+1}}^1)\) into \(\mathscr{J}=\mathscr{I}^{n+1}\mathscr{O}_Y/\mathscr{I}^{n+2}\mathscr{O}_Y\). Since \(\mathscr{J}\) is annihilated by \(\mathscr{IO}_Y\), one may then replace \(g_n^*(\mathit{\Omega}_{X_{n+1}/S_{n+1}}^1)\) by the sheaf it induces on \(Y_0\), namely \(h_0^*(\mathit{\Omega}_{X/S}^1)\), where \(h_0\) is the composite \(Y_0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y_n\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_{n+1}\), or again the composite \(Y_0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_{n+1}\), where \(g_0\colon Y_0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_0\) is induced by \(g_n\). Since the inverse image of \(\mathit{\Omega}_{X_{n+1}/S_{n+1}}^1\) on \(X_0=X_{n+1}\times_{S_{n+1}}S_0\) is \(\mathit{\Omega}_{X_{0}/S_{0}}^1\), one sees that one also has
\[ \mathscr{G}=\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}} (g_0^*(\mathit{\Omega}_{X_0/S_0}^1), \mathscr{I}^{n+1}\mathscr{O}_Y/\mathscr{I}^{n+2}\mathscr{O}_Y) \]
Thus one obtains the

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3

Let \(S,X,Y,\mathscr{I},g_n\) be as above, let \(\mathscr{P}(g_n)\) be the sheaf on \(Y\) whose sections over an open \(U\) are the prolongations \(g_{n+1}\) of \(g_n\) into an \(S_{n+1}\)-morphism \(Y_{n+1}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_{n+1}\). Then \(\mathscr{P}(g_{n})\) is a formally principal homogeneous sheaf under the sheaf of groups

\[ \mathscr{G}=\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}} (g_0^*(\mathit{\Omega}_{X_0/S_0}^1), \operatorname{gr}_{\mathscr{IO}_Y}^{n+1}(\mathscr{O}_Y)) \]
In particular:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4

If moreover \(X\) is smooth over \(S\) (at least at the points of \(g_0(Y_0)\)) then \(\mathscr{P}(g_{n})\) is even a principal homogeneous sheaf. .5cmIn particular, it defines an obstruction class in \(\mathrm{H}^1(Y_0,\mathscr{G})\), whose vanishing is necessary and sufficient for the existence of a global prolongation \(g_{n+1}\) of \(g_n\). And if such a prolongation exists, the set of all global prolongations is a principal homogeneous space under \(\mathrm{H}^0(Y_0,\mathscr{G})\). Finally, in the case considered, the sheaf \(\mathscr{G}\) can also be written

\[ \mathscr{G}=g_0^*(\mathfrak{g}_{X_0/S_0})\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}} \operatorname{gr}_{\mathscr{IO}_Y}^{n+1}(\mathscr{O}_Y) \]

Proceeding step by step, one thus sees that if all the \(\mathrm{H}^1(Y_0,\mathscr{G}_n)\) are zero (where \(\mathscr{G}_n=g_0^*(\mathfrak{g}_{X_0/S_0})\otimes \operatorname{gr}_{\mathscr{IO}_Y}^{n}(\mathscr{O}_Y)\)), then, starting with an arbitrary \(g_k\), one can successively prolong it into \(g_{k+1},\dots\) In particular, if \(\mathscr{I}\) is nilpotent, one will be able to find a prolongation \(g\) of \(g_k\) to \(Y\). The condition of vanishing of the \(\mathrm{H}^1\) is satisfied in particular if \(Y_0\) is affine. One thus finds:

blocks.html:
Corollary 5

In the statement of Theorem 1, one obtains a necessary and sufficient condition equivalent to the others by assuming that the \(Y^\prime\) which occurs in (ii) (or (ii bis)) is affine, and by requiring the existence of a global prolongation \(g\) of \(g_0\) to all of \(Y^\prime\).

\[ \operatorname{gr}^n(\mathscr{O}_Y)=\operatorname{gr}^n(\mathscr{O}_S)\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}}\mathscr{O}_{Y_0} \]

\[ \mathscr{G}_n= \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}}(g_0^*(\mathit{\Omega}_{X_0/S_0}^1),\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}) \otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}}\operatorname{gr}^n(\mathscr{O}_S), \]

blocks.html:
Corollary 6

Let \((S,X,Y,\mathscr{I},g_n)\) be as above, suppose moreover \(X\) smooth over \(S\) and \(Y\) flat over \(S\), finally \(S\) affine, and the \(\operatorname{gr}^n(\mathscr{O}_S)=\mathscr{I}^n/\mathscr{I}^{n+1}\) locally free. Under the conditions of 6, if \(g_0\) is an isomorphism the same holds for \(\widehat{g}\).
(N.B. the same result holds for closed immersions).
One thus obtains:

blocks.html:
Proposition 8

Finally, when \(\mathfrak{X}\) is a formal scheme proper over \(A\), and \(\mathfrak{Y}\) is of the form \(\widehat{Y}\) where \(Y\) is an ordinary scheme proper over \(A\), then proposition 8 gives sufficient conditions for one to be able to find an isomorphism of \(\mathfrak{X}\) with \(\widehat{Y}\), hence for the formal scheme \(\mathfrak{X}\) to be in fact “algebraic” (i.e. isomorphic to a \(\widehat{X}\), \(X\) an ordinary scheme proper over \(A\), which will then be canonically determined). pages.title: Global infinitesimal extension of smooth \(S\)-schemes
pages.html:
6 Global infinitesimal extension of smooth \(S\)-schemes

Under the conditions of theorem 1, we propose to seek whether there exists a prescheme \(X\) smooth over \(Y\) such that \(X\times_YY_0\) is \(Y_0\)-isomorphic to \(X_0\), knowing that such a scheme “exists locally over \(X_0\)”. Taking up again the method of step-by-step construction, we are led to replace \(Y\) by the letter \(S\), to assume that we are given a closed sub-prescheme \(S_0\) of \(S\) defined by a sheaf of ideals \(\mathscr{I}\), (which it is no longer necessary to assume locally nilpotent), to introduce the closed sub-preschemes \(S_n\) of \(S\) defined by the \(\mathscr{I}^{n+1}\), and to assume that we are given a sub-prescheme \(X_{n}\) smooth over \(S_n\). We propose to find an \(S_{n+1}\)-prescheme \(X_{n+1}\) “which reduces along \(X_{n}\)”, i.e. equipped with an isomorphism

\[ X_{n+1}\times_{S_{n+1}}S_n\overset{\sim}{\longleftarrow} X_n \]

which is smooth over \(S_{n+1}\) (or, what amounts to the same by II 1, flat over \(S_{n+1}\)). As we have pointed out in 4, such a datum amounts to the datum of a sheaf of algebras \(\mathscr{B}\) over \(f^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_{S_{n+1}})\) (where \(f\) is the continuous map underlying the structural morphism \(X_n\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S_n\)), equipped with an augmentation \(\mathscr{B}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{O}_{X_n}\) compatible with the augmentation \(f^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_{S_{n+1}})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_{S_n})\), and satisfying two conditions (a) and (b) which we shall not rewrite, confining ourselves to noting that they are of local nature on the topological space underlying \(X_n\). We know from 1 that a solution exists locally. It is moreover unique up to (non-unique) isomorphism, at least locally. Let us begin by making this point precise:

Proposition 1

Let \(X_{n+1}\) over \(S_{n+1}\) reduce along \(X_n\) over \(S_{n}\). Then the sheaf (on the topological space underlying \(X_{n}\), or again \(X_0\)) of the \(S_{n+1}\)-automorphisms of \(X_{n+1}\) which induce the identity on \(X_n\) is canonically isomorphic to

\[ \mathscr{G}=\mathfrak{g}_{X_0/S_0}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}} \operatorname{gr}_{\mathscr{I}}^{n+1}(\mathscr{O}_S) \]

(as a sheaf of groups).

Indeed, by 4 and 2 this sheaf is a principal homogeneous sheaf under \(\mathscr{G}\). Since it is equipped with a distinguished section (the identity automorphism of \(X_{n+1}\)), it is therefore identified as a sheaf of sets with \(\mathscr{G}\). It must be checked that this identification is compatible with the group structures. This is easy, and is moreover a particular case of a more general result on the compatibility of the structures of principal bundles, in 1 and 3, with the composition of morphisms (a result which we do not state here, but which ought to figure in the hyperplodoque).

In particular, the sheaf on \(X_0\) of germs of automorphisms of \(X_{n+1}\) (with the structures made explicit) is commutative. It follows that if \(X_{n+1}^\prime\) is another solution of the problem, isomorphic to \(X_{n+1}\) above the open set \(U\) of \(X_0\), then the isomorphism of \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Aut}}(X_{n+1})|U\) onto \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Aut}}(X_{n+1}^\prime)|U\) deduced by transport of structure from an isomorphism \(X_{n+1}|U\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} X_{n+1}^\prime|U\), does not depend on the choice of the latter. (It is moreover none other than the identity isomorphism of \(\mathscr{G}\), when one identifies both sheaves of automorphisms with \(\mathscr{G}\) by means of 1).

We deduce from 1:

Corollary 2

Let \(X_{n+1}\), \(X_{n+1}'\) be smooth over \(S_{n+1}\) and “reducing along \(X_n\)”. Then the sheaf (on the space underlying \(X_0\)) of the \(S_{n+1}\)-isomorphisms of \(X_{n+1}\) onto \(X_{n+1}'\) inducing the identity on \(X_n\), is in a natural way a principal homogeneous sheaf under \(\mathscr{G}\).

This expresses indeed that \(X_{n+1}\) and \(X_{n+1}'\) are isomorphic locally, and that the sheaf of germs of automorphisms of the former is \(\mathscr{G}\).

Let us now note that by virtue of 1, one can always find a covering \((U_i)\) of \(X_n\) by open sets (which one may assume affine), and for every \(i\) a scheme \(X^i\) smooth over \(S_{n+1}\) reducing along \(U_i\). Suppose for simplicity \(X_n\) separated, so that the \(U_{ij}=U_i\cap U_j\) are again affine open sets of \(X_n\). Since the \(\mathrm{H}^1\) of such an open set, with values in the quasi-coherent sheaf \(\mathscr{G}\), is zero, we deduce by the corollary 2 that \(X^i|U_{ij}\) is isomorphic to \(X^j|U_{ij}\); let

\[ f_{ji}:X^i|U_{ij}\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} X^j|U_{ij} \]

be such an isomorphism. It is determined up to a section of \(\mathscr{G}\) over \(U_{ij}\). Let us set, for every triple of indices:

\[ f_{ji}^{(k)}=f_{ji}|U_{ijk}\quad\text{where }U_{ijk}=U_i\cap U_j\cap U_k. \]

If we had

\[ \tag{1} {f_{kj}^{(i)}f_{ji}^{(k)}=f_{ki}^{(j)},} \]

it would follow that the \(X^i\) “glue together” by the \(f_{ji}\), and hence that they define a solution \(X=X_{n+1}\) of the problem sought. Such a solution exists more generally, if one can modify the \(f_{ji}\) into \(f_{ji}'\):

\[ \tag{2} {f_{ji}'=f_{ji}g_{ji}\quad (g_{ji}\in\Gamma(U_{ij},\mathscr{G}))} \]

in such a way that the \(f_{ji}'\) satisfy the condition of transitivity above. This sufficient condition for the existence of a solution is also necessary, as one sees by recalling that such a solution \(X\) must, on each \(U_i\), be isomorphic to \(X^i\), which thus allows one to choose isomorphisms

\[ f_i:X|U_i\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} X^i \]

and to define

\[ f_{ji}'=(f_j|U_{ij})(f_i|U_{ij})^{-1}:X^i|U_{ij}\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} X^j|U_{ij} \]

satisfying the gluing condition.

Now let us set

\[ \tag{3} {f_{ijk}=(f_{ki}^{(j)})^{-1}f_{kj}^{(i)}f_{ji}^{(k)},} \]

it is an automorphism of \(X^i|U_{ijk}\), which we shall identify with a section of \(\mathscr{G}\) by means of 1. One verifies that it is a \(2\)-cocycle \(f\) of the open covering \(\mathscr{U}=(U_i)\), with coefficients in \(\mathscr{G}\), by a little formal computation left to the reader. The same computation shows that by means of (2), the gluing condition (1) for the \(f_{ij}'\) is equivalent to the formula

\[ \tag{4} {f=dg,} \]

where \(g=(g_{ij})\) is regarded as a \(1\)-cochain of \(\mathscr{U}\) with coefficients in \(\mathscr{G}\). Hence the necessary and sufficient condition for the existence of a solution of the problem is that the cohomology class in \(\mathrm{H}^2(\mathscr{U},\mathscr{G})\) defined by the cocycle (3) be zero. Let us note moreover that since \(\mathscr{U}=(U_i)\) is an affine covering of \(X_0\) which is a scheme, \(\mathrm{H}^2(\mathscr{U},\mathscr{G})\) is identified with \(\mathrm{H}^2(X_0,\mathscr{G})\). It is moreover immediate that the cohomology class thus obtained in \(\mathrm{H}^2(X_0,\mathscr{G})\) does not depend on the affine covering considered. It will be called the obstruction class to the extension of \(X_n\) into a scheme \(X_{n+1}\) smooth over \(S_{n+1}\).

Suppose this obstruction zero. Then a reasoning sketched above shows that every solution \(X=X_{n+1}\) is isomorphic to a solution obtained by gluing from isomorphisms \(f_{ji}'\), which one may assume of the form (2), the gluing condition being none other than (3). The set of admissible \(g\) is thus a principal homogeneous space under the group \(\mathrm{Z}^1(\mathscr{U},\mathscr{G})\) of \(1\)-cocycles of \(\mathscr{U}\) with coefficients in \(\mathscr{G}\). Moreover, one verifies at once that two cochains \(g\) and \(g'\) (such that \(dg=dg'=f\)) define isomorphic solutions if and only if the cocycle \(g-g'\) is of the form \(dh\), where \(h=(h_i)\in\mathrm{C}^0(\mathscr{U},\mathscr{G})\). We thus find:

Theorem 3

Let \((S,\mathscr{I},X_n)\) be as above, \(X_n\) being assumed separated1. Then one can canonically define an obstruction class in \(\mathrm{H}^2(X_0,\mathscr{G})\) (where \(\mathscr{G}\) is defined in 1), whose vanishing is necessary and sufficient for the existence of a scheme \(X_{n+1}\) smooth over \(S_{n+1}\) reducing along \(X_n\). If this obstruction is zero, then the set of classes, up to isomorphism (inducing the identity on \(X_n\)) of \(S_{n+1}\)-preschemes \(X_{n+1}\) reducing along \(X_n\), is in a natural way a principal homogeneous space under \(\mathrm{H}^1(X_0,\mathscr{G})\).

Remarks 4

Starting from 1, the arguments made here are purely formal, and are advantageously transcribed within the framework of local categories, or even of general fibred categories. The obstruction class to the existence of a “global” object of a category (of which one can find an object “locally”, and of which two objects are always “locally isomorphic”, the group of automorphisms of any object being commutative) thus obtained in the general context contains as a particular case the “second boundary homomorphism” in an exact sequence of sheaves of not-necessarily-commutative groups, (studied for example by Grothendieck in Kansas or Tôhoku). The silly little computation by cocycles made here must therefore be regarded as a makeshift, due to the non-existence of a satisfactory reference text.

5

One will note that in 3, there is in general no privileged element in the principal homogeneous space considered under \(\mathrm{H}^1(X_0,\mathscr{G})\). This is reflected in particular by the fact that one obtains (localizing on \(S\)) a principal homogeneous sheaf over \(S_0\), with structural group \(\mathrm{R}^1f_*(\mathscr{G})\), which is not necessarily trivial, i.e. which defines a cohomology class in \(\mathrm{H}^1(S_0,\mathrm{R}^1f_*(\mathscr{G}))\) which is not necessarily zero. (When one assumes that the class \(d\in\mathrm{H}^2(X_0,\mathscr{G})\) is not zero, but is zero “locally above \(S\)”, i.e. defines a zero section of \(\mathrm{R}^2f_*(\mathscr{G})\), i.e. a zero element in \(\mathrm{H}^0(S_0,\mathrm{R}^2f_*(\mathscr{G}))\)).

6

For the moment one knows almost nothing about the general algebraic mechanism of the cohomology classes introduced in this article and its relations with the preceding article, and one can say nothing precise about it in the simplest particular cases, such as the case of abelian schemes over Artinian rings2. One hopes that there will be people to slog away at the question, which seems particularly interesting. It is intimately connected in particular with the “theory of moduli” of algebraic structures.

Corollary 7

Suppose that \(\mathrm{H}^2(X_0,\mathscr{G})=0\), then an \(X_{n+1}\) exists, and it is unique up to isomorphism if moreover \(\mathrm{H}^1(X_0,\mathscr{G})=0\).

In particular, one concludes from this, proceeding step by step (and noting that an affine scheme is acyclic for a quasi-coherent sheaf):

Corollary 8

Under the conditions of Theorem 1, if \(X_0\) is affine, then there exists an \(X\) smooth over \(Y\) reducing along \(X_0\), and this \(X\) is unique up to (non-unique) isomorphism.

One will note that the direct proof of Th. 1 could not give us this result.

Corollary 9

Under the conditions of 3, suppose \(S\) affine with ring \(A\), \(\mathscr{I}\) defined by an ideal \(I\) of \(A\), and finally the \(\operatorname{gr}^n_{\mathscr{I}}(\mathscr{O}_S)=\mathscr{I}^n/\mathscr{I}^{n+1}\) locally free. Then \(\mathrm{H}^i(X_0,\mathscr{G})\) is identified with \(\mathrm{H}^i(X_0,\mathscr{G}_0)\otimes_A\operatorname{gr}^{n+1}_I(A)\), where

\[ \mathscr{G}_0=\mathfrak{g}_{X_0/S_0}, \]

hence the obstruction class to the prolongation of \(X_n\) lies in \(\mathrm{H}^2(X_0,\mathscr{G}_0)\otimes_A\operatorname{gr}^{n+1}_I(A)\), and if it is zero, the set of classes (up to isomorphism) of solutions is a principal homogeneous space under \(\mathrm{H}^1(X_0,\mathscr{G}_0)\otimes_A\operatorname{gr}^{n+1}_I(A)\).

In particular:

Corollary 10

Under the conditions of 9 suppose

\[ \mathrm{H}^2(X_0,\mathfrak{g}_{X_0/S_0})=0, \]

then there exists a formal \(\widehat{I}\)-adic scheme \(\mathfrak{X}\) over the formal \(\mathscr{I}\)-adic completion \(\widehat{S}\) of \(S\), which is “smooth over \(S\)” (i.e. the \(\mathfrak{X}_p\) are smooth over the \(S_p\)) and which reduces along \(X_n\), i.e. equipped with an isomorphism

\[ \mathfrak{X}\times_S S_n\overset{\sim}{\longleftarrow} X_n. \]

If moreover \(\mathrm{H}^1(X_0,\mathfrak{g}_{X_0/S_0})=0\), then such an \(\mathfrak{X}\) is unique up to isomorphism.

Indeed, one constructs step by step \(X_{n+1}\), \(X_{n+2}\), etc., whence \(\mathfrak{X}\) by passing to the direct limit of the \(X_i\). The uniqueness assertion already appears in the preceding one.

7 Application to the construction of formal schemes and of ordinary smooth schemes over a complete local ring \(A\)

The results of the preceding section sometimes allow one to prove the existence of an \(\mathfrak{m}\)-adic formal scheme over such a ring, which reduces to a given smooth scheme \(X_0\) over \(k\). Let us distinguish two cases:

a) \(A\) is “of equal characteristics” (this is in particular the case if \(k\) is of characteristic \(0\)). Then it is known that there exists a subfield of representatives of \(A\), i.e. a subfield \(k'\) such that \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} k\) induces an isomorphism \(k'\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} k\). Then there even exists an ordinary smooth scheme over \(A\) which reduces to \(X_0\), namely \(X=X_0\otimes_kA\), \(A\) being regarded as an algebra over \(k\) by means of the homomorphism \(k\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} k'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A\) defined by \(k'\). It must however be noted that this construction is not “natural”; it is easy to convince oneself (already in the case where \(A=k[t]/(t^2)\), the algebra of dual numbers) that another lifting homomorphism \(k\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A\) (defined in this instance by an absolute derivation of \(k\) into itself) defines an \(X'\) over \(A\) which in general is not isomorphic to \(X\) (if \(\mathrm{H}^1(X_0,\mathfrak{g}_{X_0/k})\neq0\)). It would moreover be interesting to study (for \(k\) of characteristic \(0\), or non-perfect and of characteristic \(p>0\)) which are the \(X\) smooth over \(A\) that are obtained in this way, under what condition two homomorphisms \(k\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A\) define isomorphic \(A\)-schemes. Nevertheless, the existence of \(k'\) suffices to imply that the first obstruction to the lifting of \(X_0\), which lies in \(\mathrm{H}^2(X_0,\mathfrak{g}_{X_0/k})\otimes_k\mathfrak{m}/\mathfrak{m}^2\), is necessarily zero. Of course, once one has then lifted \(X_0\) to an \(X_1\) smooth over \(A/\mathfrak{m}^2\), the new obstruction to the construction of \(X_2\) will in general not be zero: it will be a function of a variable element in a certain principal homogeneous space under \(\mathrm{H}^1(X_0,\mathscr{G}_0)\otimes\mathfrak{m}/\mathfrak{m}^2\) and lies in \(\mathrm{H}^2(X_0,\mathscr{G}_0)\otimes\mathfrak{m}^2/\mathfrak{m}^3\): it would be advisable to study the situation in a detailed way1.

b) \(A\) is of unequal characteristics. In this case, one knows nothing, (except if by chance \(\mathrm{H}^2(X_0,\mathfrak{g}_{X_0/k})=0\), in which case one can construct a simple \(\mathfrak{m}\)-adic formal scheme over \(A\) which reduces to \(k\)). Even if \(A=\mathbf{Z}/p^2\mathbf{Z}\) and if \(X_0\) is an “abelian” scheme of dimension \(2\), one does not know whether it can be lifted to an \(X=X_1\) smooth over \(A\)2; on the other hand, one has no example of an \(X_0\) which has been proved not to come from an ordinary scheme \(X\) smooth over \(A\). (I have the impression that this must exist, with \(X_0\) a projective surface)3. Let us simply point out that according to Cohen's theorem, there exists a Cohen \(p\)-ring \(B\) with residue field \(k\) and a homomorphism \(B\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A\) inducing the identity isomorphism on the residue fields; consequently, the “strongest” lifting result would be obtained by taking for \(A\) a Cohen \(p\)-ring: if there exists a solution (ordinary or formal) over such a ring, there exists one over every complete local ring with residue field \(k\). In particular, since for a Cohen \(p\)-ring \(\mathfrak{m}/\mathfrak{m}^2\) is canonically identified with \(k\), one sees that for every smooth scheme \(X_0\) over a field \(k\) of characteristic \(p>0\), there exists a cohomology class in \(\mathrm{H}^2(X_0,\mathfrak{g}_{X_0/k})\), the first obstruction to the lifting of \(X_0\) to a scheme smooth over a Cohen \(p\)-ring; one does not know whether it can be non-zero4.

Even if one manages, step by step, to construct the \(X_n\) which reduce to \(X_0\), this in general yields only a formal scheme \(\mathfrak{X}\) smooth over \(A\), which reduces to \(X_0\). When \(X_0\) is proper over \(A\), there remains the question whether \(\mathfrak{X}\) is in fact algebraizable, so as to be able to obtain an ordinary scheme proper over \(A\) and simple over \(A\), which reduces to \(X_0\). The only known criterion (pointed out in the Séminaire Bourbaki, and which appears in the Éléments, Chap. III, 4.7.1) is the following: if \(\mathfrak{X}\) is proper over \(A\), and if \(\mathscr{L}\) is an invertible sheaf on \(\mathfrak{X}\) such that the induced sheaf \(\mathscr{L}_0\) on \(X_0\) is ample (i.e. a suitable tensor power \(\mathscr{L}_0^{\otimes n}\), \(n>0\), arises from a projective immersion of \(X_0\)) then there exists a scheme \(X\) projective over \(A\), and an ample invertible sheaf on \(X\), such that \((\mathfrak{X},\mathscr{L})\) is deduced from it by \(\mathfrak{m}\)-adic completion. This therefore leads us, given a locally free sheaf \(\mathscr{E}_0\) on \(X_0\) (which we shall choose invertible ample for our purpose), to extend it to a locally free sheaf \(\mathscr{E}\) on \(\mathfrak{X}\). For this, one is reduced to constructing step by step locally free sheaves \(\mathscr{E}_n\) on the \(X_n\). The discussion is entirely analogous to that of 6, (cf. remark 4), the essential role being played by the sheaf of automorphisms of an \(\mathscr{E}_{n+1}\) which induce the identity on \(\mathscr{E}_n\): one shows at once that this sheaf is identified with

\[ \tag{*}{} \mathscr{G}= \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{O}_{X_0}}(\mathscr{E}_0, \mathscr{E}_0\otimes\operatorname{gr}_{\mathscr{I}}^{n+1}(\mathscr{O}_X))= \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{O}_{X_0}}(\mathscr{E}_0, \mathscr{E}_0)\otimes\operatorname{gr}_{\mathscr{I}}^{n+1}(\mathscr{O}_X) \]

which is again a sheaf of commutative groups. One finds:

Proposition 1

Let \(S\) be a prescheme equipped with a quasi-coherent sheaf of ideals \(\mathscr{I}\), \(X\) a prescheme over \(S\), \(S_n\) the subprescheme of \(S\) defined by \(\mathscr{I}^{n+1}\), and \(X_n=X\times_SS_n\) (for every integer \(n\)). Let \(\mathscr{E}_n\) be a locally free sheaf on \(X_n\); we propose to extend it to a locally free sheaf \(\mathscr{E}_{n+1}\) on \(X_{n+1}\). Then \(\mathscr{E}_n\) defines a canonical obstruction class in \(\mathrm{H}^2(X_0,\mathscr{G})\), where \(\mathscr{G}\) is the quasi-coherent sheaf given by the formula above, a class whose vanishing is necessary and sufficient for the existence of an \(\mathscr{E}_{n+1}\) extending \(\mathscr{E}_n\). If this class is zero, then the set of classes, up to isomorphism (inducing the identity on \(\mathscr{E}_n\)), of solutions \(\mathscr{E}_{n+1}\), is a principal homogeneous space under \(\mathrm{H}^1(X_0,\mathscr{G})\).

This proposition gives rise to the usual corollaries. Let us point out only that if \(X\) is flat over \(S\), then one can write

\[ \mathscr{G}= \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{O}_{X_0}}(\mathscr{E}_0, \mathscr{E}_0) \otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}} \operatorname{gr}_{\mathscr{I}}^{n+1}(\mathscr{O}_S) \]

whence, if \(S\) is affine with ring \(A\) and if the \(\mathscr{I}^n/\mathscr{I}^{n+1}\) are locally free, the sufficient condition

\[ \mathrm{H}^2(X_0,\mathscr{G}_0)=0 \qquad\text{with}\qquad \mathscr{G}_0=\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{O}_{X_0}}(\mathscr{E}_0, \mathscr{E}_0) \]

for the existence of an \(\mathscr{E}_{n+1}\), hence step by step for the existence of successive extensions \(\mathscr{E}_m\) (\(m=n,n+1,\) etc.).

Returning to the initial situation, one thus finds:

Proposition 2

Let \(A\) be a complete local ring, \(\mathfrak{X}\) a formal scheme proper and flat over \(A\), such that \(X_0\) is projective and that \(\mathrm{H}^2(X_0,\mathscr{O}_{X_0})=0\). Then there exists a scheme \(X\) projective over \(A\) whose \(\mathfrak{m}\)-adic formal completion is isomorphic to \(\mathfrak{X}\).

Combining with 10, one finds:

Theorem 3

Let \(A\) be a complete local ring with residue field \(k\), \(X_0\) a scheme projective and smooth over \(k\), such that

\[ \mathrm{H}^2(X_0,\mathfrak{g}_{X_0/k})=\mathrm{H}^2(X_0,\mathscr{O}_{X_0})=0 \]

Then there exists a scheme \(X\) smooth and projective over \(A\), which reduces to \(X_0\).

More generally, if one is given an \(X_n\) smooth over \(A_n=A/\mathfrak{m}^{n+1}\) which reduces to \(X_0\), then there exists an \(X\) smooth and proper over \(A\) and an isomorphism \(X\otimes_AA_n=X_n\).

Corollary 4

Every smooth and proper curve over \(k\) arises by reduction from a smooth and proper curve over \(A\).

It is this result that will be the essential tool (together with the existence theorem for sheaves in Formal Geometry) for studying the fundamental group of \(X_0\) by transcendental means.
